title: Compact Kubernetes resource explorer with tree+table hybrid and inline pod logs
design_brief: You're building the resource explorer for a Kubernetes management UI (think Lens, K9s, Headlamp). SREs need to see hundreds of pods, deployments, services at once and drill into any of them in 2 keystrokes. Pain point: most K8s UIs are either web ports of kubectl (just dumps of YAML) or pretty but slow tile dashboards that hide the data. The bar: K9s (the gold standard for keyboard-driven density), Lens at its best, Headlamp's resource views.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Each resource type on its own tab (pods/deployments/services as separate views — kills correlation)
Expanding rows that push siblings down by 400px
Status as a colored emoji 🟢 🔴 — looks unprofessional and is hard to scan
Modal YAML editor that takes 80% of viewport
Generic search box that only filters by name
Auto-refresh every 5s that disrupts user reading
ux_reasoning:
Kubernetes has dozens of resource types but they all share three queryable axes: namespace, status, and labels. The dominant UX failure is treating resource types as the primary navigation when actually NAMESPACE is. K9s gets this right; we follow.

Layout: left rail is namespace selector (8px-wide colored stripe per active namespace, click to focus, Shift+click for multi-select). Main area is a table with columns adaptable per resource type but with consistent gutter columns: name, status, age, namespace, ready/desired. Top of main has a kind selector pill row (pods · deployments · services · ingresses · ...) that filters the table to that kind.

Density moves: row height 22px (extreme density, K9s level). Status is encoded as a 6px square next to name (filled = healthy, hollow = pending, X = failed). NEVER an emoji. Age column uses compact format (2d, 5h, 3m). Numeric columns are tabular-nums right-aligned.

Inline pod logs: pressing Enter on a pod row reveals a 240px-tall log pane BELOW that row (other rows compress proportionally with FLIP — same pattern as the cohort heatmap from batch 01). Logs stream in real-time, with the row's existing keyboard nav still active (j/k moves to next pod, log pane reflows to that pod). This is the killer move: log inspection without leaving the resource table.

Live refresh: data updates over websocket. Updated cells get a 1-frame border flash (16ms, the order-book trick). Cells the user is currently focused on PAUSE updating — never overwrite content under a focused cursor. Resume on blur.

Label chips: each resource has labels visible as tiny gray chips at the end of the row. Click a chip to add it as a filter (the log-payload pivot pattern). Multiple chip filters AND together.

Keyboard model: : enters command mode (k9s style — :pods enters pod kind, :ns prod selects namespace), / filters, Enter expands log/yaml, e edits, d describes, ctrl+l clears focus. Vim-style; learnable in an afternoon.

Rejected: a graph view of resource relationships. Useful in theory; in incidents you want a table you can scan, not a graph you have to interpret. Also rejected: AI assistant chat in the sidebar. Adds noise; the actual UX win is fast keyboard nav + correlated data, not natural-language search.

design_tokens.colors: Status indicators: healthy oklch(0.6 0.14 145), pending oklch(0.75 0.16 80) outline only, failed oklch(0.6 0.18 25) with X glyph. Namespace stripes: 12-color palette assigned deterministically by namespace name hash. Row hover: oklch(0.97 0.005 250). Selected row: oklch(0.94 0.04 250). Label chips: oklch(0.55 0.005 250) on transparent, 10px.
design_tokens.typography: All content: 'Berkeley Mono', 'JetBrains Mono' 11.5px. Headers: Inter 10px uppercase tracking-wider. Numbers: tabular-nums.
design_tokens.spacing: Row height: 22px. Status indicator: 6px square at 12px from left edge. Label chips: 4px gap, 2px padding. Namespace rail: 8px wide.
design_tokens.motion: Row update flash: 16ms border. Inline log expand: 250ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). NO transitions on text content.

component_architecture: <ResourceExplorer<TKind extends ResourceKind>> generic. <NamespaceRail /> + <KindFilter /> + <ResourceTable /> + <InlineDetail /> stacked. State: { focusedRowId, expandedRowId, kindFilter, labelFilters[], pausedRows: Set<string> }. Websocket updates flow through a useLiveResources() hook that writes to a refs-based store and triggers minimal rerenders (rows with changed data only).
interaction_details: j/k or arrows: move focus through rows. Enter: expand inline log pane below focused pod (other rows compress with FLIP). j/k while expanded: log pane reflows to next pod.
Click label chip in any row: adds as filter. Multiple chips AND together. Click chip in filter row to remove.
: enters command mode (k9s-style — :ns prod selects namespace). / enters search mode. e edits selected resource YAML in-place.
Live updates: changed cells flash a 1-frame border in their status color (16ms). Cell currently focused never overwrites — pauses live update on that cell only.
Namespace rail: click toggles single, Shift+click multi-selects. Each namespace gets a stable hue from name hash.
accessibility_considerations: role='grid' on root, role='row' / aria-selected on rows. Status glyphs have aria-label (healthy/pending/failed) — not just color, also shape (filled / hollow / X). Namespace rail is a real <nav>. Inline log region uses aria-live='polite' aria-relevant='additions'. Keyboard: full nav without mouse. Reduced-motion: log expand becomes instant. Color: namespace hashes ensure deterministic colors per namespace name; supplemented by the namespace name text always being visible.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Inline log pane expands below the focused pod with FLIP compression of other rows — log inspection without leaving the resource table or losing context. 2) Status glyphs use SHAPE (filled square, hollow square, X) not color alone — readable in any theme or by colorblind users. 3) Live updates pause on cells the user is focused on — never overwrite content under the cursor during reading.

title: Email triage with single-key actions and Gmail-style keyboard shortcut grammar
design_brief: You're building an email triage view for a focused email client (think Superhuman, HEY, Shortwave). Power users want to process 100+ emails in 5 minutes. Pain point: most email UIs require constant mouse interaction or have shortcuts that are random letters with no system. The bar: Superhuman (the gold standard for keyboard-driven email), Gmail's classic shortcut model, mutt.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Buttons in a toolbar that have no keyboard equivalent
Modal compose window that loses keyboard context
'Mark as read' as a separate action requiring multiple keys
Hover-only quick actions on rows
Inconsistent shortcut grammar (e for archive, R for reply, but Cmd+Shift+S for snooze)
Tooltips on every shortcut that linger long after learning
ux_reasoning:
Email shortcuts must form a GRAMMAR, not a list. Verb-noun patterns let users compose actions without memorizing 50 keys. Superhuman's genius is teaching this grammar without ever explaining it.

The grammar: single-letter verbs (e = archive, # = delete, r = reply, R = reply all, f = forward, l = label, s = snooze, b = boost-priority, y = mark read/unread, m = mute thread, j/k = nav, gi = go to inbox, gs = go to starred). Capital letters are 'extended' versions of lowercase (R = reply all is reply to a wider audience).

Chording: gi / gs / gd are two-key chords (g + destination). Combo presses within 800ms execute the chord; otherwise the first key is treated as a typo and cleared. This is Gmail's original shortcut model, refined.

Visible without being noisy: when the user focuses a row, a hint strip at the bottom of the screen shows the 5 most common actions ('E archive · # delete · R reply · S snooze · L label'). After 7 days of usage, the hint strip auto-hides — at that point the user has learned. Re-shows for unfamiliar actions context.

Snooze interaction: pressing s opens a tiny inline popover with 4 time presets (later today, tomorrow, this weekend, next week) and a typed-input fallback ('in 3 days'). Numbers 1-4 select presets directly. This is Superhuman's snooze popover, the cleanest in the business.

Undo: every action has a 5-second undo window via a single keystroke (z = undo). Subtle bottom-of-screen pill shows 'Archived · z to undo'. Disappears silently after 5s.

Thread reading: opening a thread (Enter or o) loads it INLINE in a split view (left = list still navigable with j/k, right = thread). This means triaging doesn't require leaving the list. Esc closes thread; o again toggles.

Motion: zero on row state changes. Archive instantly removes the row; next row slides up to take its place over 100ms (the only animation, and it's purposive — communicates removal). NEVER spring on email triage. Speed is the value.

Rejected: a 'Command palette' for actions. Power users don't want to type 4 keys when they could press 1. The palette is for novices; we serve the experts. Also rejected: voice control. Email triage is silent; voice would be intrusive in the workflow.

design_tokens.colors: Row default: oklch(0.99 0.005 250). Focused row: 2px left border in oklch(0.55 0.18 250), bg oklch(0.97 0.005 250). Unread: bold weight + 4px dot in oklch(0.55 0.18 250). Hint strip: oklch(0.18 0.005 250 / 0.92) bg with white text. Undo pill: same.
design_tokens.typography: Subject: Inter 14px medium. Sender: 13px regular. Preview: 13px regular oklch(0.55). Hint strip kbd: 'JetBrains Mono' 11px in oklch(0.9 0.005 250).
design_tokens.spacing: Row height: 48px. Hint strip: 32px fixed at bottom. Snooze popover: 220px wide.
design_tokens.motion: Row removal: 100ms ease-out + collapse. Hint strip slide-in: 80ms opacity. Undo pill enter: 120ms opacity. NO spring.

component_architecture: <EmailTriage<TThread>> generic. State: focused thread id, open thread id, snooze popover open, undo queue (FIFO max 5). <ShortcutEngine /> is a single keydown listener at root that interprets the grammar (handles chord buffering with 800ms timer). <HintStrip /> reads the current focused row + user's hint-shown-count to decide visibility. <UndoPill /> renders the topmost undo entry.
interaction_details: j/k or arrows: move focus through threads. Enter or o: open focused thread in right pane (list stays navigable).
E archives focused thread; focus moves to next; undo pill appears at bottom with 5s z-to-undo window.
s opens inline snooze popover at focused row; 1-4 selects preset, typing a duration commits.
g + i: go to inbox. g + s: go to starred. Chord must complete within 800ms.
Hint strip at bottom shows 5 most common verbs. After 7 days of usage, auto-hides — user has learned the grammar.
accessibility_considerations: role='listbox' / role='option' / aria-selected for the thread list. Unread state announced via aria-label='unread' on the dot AND via font-weight change (not color alone). Shortcut grammar respects input focus — never intercepts typing in inputs. Undo pill uses role='status' aria-live='polite'. All shortcut actions have menu-bar equivalents (the menu bar IS the keyboard help). Reduced-motion: row removal becomes instant, undo pill appears without slide. Color: focused row has both bg shift AND 2px left border; unread has both dot AND bold weight.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Shortcuts form a GRAMMAR (single-char verbs, capital extensions, g-chord for navigation) — learnable in an afternoon, scales to dozens of actions. 2) Hint strip self-hides after 7 days of usage — the help is temporary, the speed is permanent. 3) Undo is a single keystroke (z) on a 5s window — every action is reversible without leaving the keyboard.

title: Async video review with timeline pins and comment-attached frame seeking
design_brief: You're building the video review tool for a creative agency platform (think Frame.io, Vimeo Review, Wipster). Designers/clients comment on specific frames; reviewers respond async. Pain point: most tools put comments in a sidebar without spatial connection to the timeline, and frame-seeking is laggy/imprecise. The bar: Frame.io's comment-pinned timeline, Loom's comment threading on video.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Comments in sidebar disconnected from timeline
Click-to-seek that only goes to nearest second
Per-frame markers that overlap into a single fat bar at high comment density
Generic 'Reply' button for each comment without thread visualization
Auto-play next when video ends — interrupts reviewer flow
Tooltip preview frames that take 800ms to load and show wrong frames
ux_reasoning:
Video review's core unit is the FRAME-COMMENT pair. The UI must make that pairing inescapable: every comment shows its frame thumbnail; every timeline pin shows its comment count.

Timeline pins: each comment is a 4px vertical bar at its timestamp position on the timeline (scrubber bar). Pins cluster when adjacent (within 8px) — collapsed cluster shows a count badge. Hovering a pin/cluster: a small popover above the timeline shows the FIRST line of comment(s) + frame thumbnail. Click pin: video seeks to that frame, comment scrolls into focus in the sidebar.

Frame thumbnails: 24x14 pixel images generated server-side (every 2s of video). Loaded into a sprite sheet — single network request for all thumbnails. Hover anywhere on the timeline scrub track: thumbnail follows cursor showing the frame at that timestamp. This is the YouTube preview, refined: thumbnail position lerps for smoothness (60Hz).

Frame-accurate seeking: clicking a comment pin doesn't seek to the nearest second — it seeks to the exact frame (assuming 24fps, ~41ms precision). We track video frame rate metadata and use video.currentTime with millisecond precision.

Comment thread visualization: each top-level comment in the sidebar shows the frame thumbnail in its header, the comment body, and an inline reply chain (max 5 visible, 'View N more' link). Replies are visually nested (12px indent) but minimally — heavy nesting becomes unreadable.

Drawing on frames: clicking the video while paused enters annotation mode (cursor becomes crosshair, dragging draws a colored box). On release, a comment composer appears with the drawing as context. Each annotation has the commenter's color. This is Frame.io's killer feature.

Motion: scrubbing the video shows comments fade in/out as their pins enter/leave the visible window. NO bounce, NO spring. The video should always feel like the protagonist.

Rejected: AI-generated transcript with timestamps as primary comment surface. Useful but secondary; for VIDEO review, the visual frame is the spatial reference. Also rejected: 'pin to scene change' auto-suggestion. Reviewers want to pin where THEY think matters, not where an edge detector thinks.

design_tokens.colors: Timeline track: oklch(0.85 0.005 250). Buffered bar: oklch(0.7 0.005 250). Playhead: oklch(0.55 0.18 250). Comment pin: per-commenter color from the standard 12-palette. Cluster badge: oklch(0.2 0.01 250) bg with white count.
design_tokens.typography: Comment body: Inter 14px / 22px. Commenter name: 13px medium. Timestamp on comment: 11px tabular-nums oklch(0.55). Thumbnail timecode overlay: 10px mono oklch(0.99).
design_tokens.spacing: Timeline height: 32px. Pin width: 4px (8px tap target with invisible padding). Thumbnail: 96x54 (16:9). Comment row: 8px padding, 4px gap between replies.
design_tokens.motion: Thumbnail follow on scrub: 60Hz lerp (0.4 factor). Pin appearance: instant. Comment-seek smooth scroll in sidebar: 200ms ease-out. NO video-related springs.

component_architecture: <VideoReview<TComment>> generic. <VideoPlayer /> with custom <Timeline /> using <Pin /> children. <CommentSidebar /> renders threads via <Thread /> with <Reply /> children. State: { currentTimeMs, comments[], selectedCommentId, drawingMode }. Pins and sidebar are bound: clicking either selects the comment and seeks the video. Thumbnails come from a sprite sheet via background-position.
interaction_details: Hover timeline: thumbnail follows cursor at 60Hz showing the frame at that timestamp from sprite sheet (single network request for all thumbs).
Click pin or cluster: video seeks to exact frame (millisecond precision via video.currentTime), corresponding comment scrolls into focus in sidebar.
Click frame thumbnail in comment sidebar: video seeks to that comment's frame. Both directions of binding work.
Click video while paused: enters annotation mode (crosshair cursor); drag draws colored box; release opens comment composer with drawing as context.
Pins cluster within 8px on the timeline; cluster shows count badge above. Hover cluster: tiny popover with first comment + thumbnail.
accessibility_considerations: Each pin is a real <button> with aria-label including comment count + timestamp. Timeline supports keyboard nav: arrow keys move playhead by 1s, Shift+arrow by 10s, Cmd+arrow jumps between comment pins. Each comment thread is a semantic <article> with proper heading hierarchy. Frame thumbnails in sidebar are buttons with aria-label='Seek to 12.3 seconds'. Reduced-motion: thumbnail-follow becomes snap-to-cursor; smooth-scroll in sidebar becomes instant. Color: pins are supplemented by author name in aria-label; clusters show numeric count.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Frame thumbnails in sprite sheet (single image, background-position scrubbing) — instant preview at any timestamp, zero per-frame requests. 2) Bidirectional pin↔sidebar binding: click either, both react — the comment and the frame are inseparable. 3) Click-and-drag on paused video draws colored annotations that anchor comments to image regions — Frame.io's killer feature, implemented in pure DOM.

title: Send-payment confirmation with bank-grade trust theater and atomic transition
design_brief: You're building the send-money confirmation step for a fintech app (think Wise, Revolut, Robinhood Cash, Square Cash). Pain point: most fintech 'Send' buttons either fire instantly (terrifying for first-time users) or use the same modal confirm pattern for $5 and $5000 (no calibrated trust theater). The bar: Apple Pay's authentication flow, Wise's send confirmation, Robinhood's stock buy.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Generic 'Are you sure? Yes/No' modal
Loading spinner that runs for 3s after Confirm
Success page with confetti
Slide-to-confirm where the slider has the same friction at $1 and $10k
Disabled Send button until you check 'I confirm' box
Toast notification 'Payment sent' after Confirm — there's no in-context feedback
ux_reasoning:
Sending money is irreversible. The interaction must do TWO things at once: communicate 'this is final' (trust theater) and 'this happened' (atomic feedback). Most apps do neither well.

Deliberate friction calibrated to amount: amounts under $50 → single click confirms after a 1.5s 'press and hold' (haptic at half, completion at full). Amounts $50-$1000 → press-and-hold for 2.5s. Amounts over $1000 → biometric (Face ID / Touch ID on mobile, OS auth on desktop). This is the Apple Pay model adapted to web: friction scales with stakes.

The press-and-hold visualization: a horizontal progress fills the Send button from left to right as the user holds. Release before completion: bar recedes over 200ms (the swipe-to-archive cancel pattern from batch 01). Reach completion: a single haptic tap + the button morphs into a 'sent' confirmation state — checkmark icon draws in over 300ms, button color shifts from action-blue to success-green, the amount + recipient lock into the post-send display.

Atomic transition: the send button doesn't become a spinner. The button IS the confirmation. After completion, it morphs in place: 'Send $42.00 to Alice' → 'Sent · $42.00 to Alice'. No page navigation, no modal close. The user's eye stays where they last looked.

Failure case: if the server rejects (insufficient funds, declined), the button morphs to a third state: red bg, X icon, message 'Couldn't send · Insufficient funds — Add funds →'. Action button inline with the error. Trust comes from never losing context.

Receipt: 4s after success, a small 'View receipt' link slides in below the morphed button. Non-blocking; user can dismiss or open in a side panel.

Motion: the only animations are the progress fill (linear during hold), the morph between states (300ms cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1) for color + icon), and the receipt slide-in (200ms ease-out). Every animation is on the same primitive (the button). The user's attention is anchored.

Rejected: a confirmation modal with 'Are you sure?'. Modals fragment attention; the action and its confirmation should share a surface. Also rejected: a separate 'Sending...' state with a spinner inside the button. Spinners are honest about uncertainty, but here we have the press-and-hold AS the uncertainty period; a spinner after that is redundant tax.

design_tokens.colors: Send state bg: oklch(0.55 0.18 250). Progress fill: oklch(0.4 0.18 250) overlay 30%. Success state bg: oklch(0.55 0.16 145). Failure state bg: oklch(0.6 0.18 25). Text: white on all states. Disabled (e.g., insufficient validation): oklch(0.85 0.005 250) bg + oklch(0.55 0.005 250) text.
design_tokens.typography: Button text: Inter 15px medium. Amount inside button: tabular-nums. Receipt link: 13px regular underline.
design_tokens.spacing: Button height: 48px (large enough for a confident press). Padding: 16px horizontal. Receipt link: 12px above button after success.
design_tokens.motion: Progress fill during hold: linear (real-time). Release recede: 200ms ease-out. State morph: 300ms cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1) for color + icon. Receipt slide-in: 200ms ease-out + 4px translateY. Haptic: single 8ms tap at 50% of hold.

component_architecture: <SendButton<TAmount>> generic over amount type. Props: amount, currency, recipient, threshold mapping, onSend (async fn returning success or error). Internal state machine: 'idle' → 'holding' → 'sending' → 'sent' | 'failed'. usePressAndHold() hook handles the gesture (with abort on release/cancel). Biometric path uses WebAuthn for >threshold amounts.
interaction_details: Press and hold the Send button: a horizontal bar fills from left to right at constant speed. Single 8ms haptic at 50%.
Release before completion: bar recedes over 200ms (action cancelled cleanly).
Reach completion: button morphs in-place — color shifts to green over 300ms, check icon draws in, text changes to 'Sent · $X to Y'.
Amounts over $1000: button shows 'Authenticate to send $X' instead of hold; tap triggers OS biometric.
Failure: button morphs to red with X icon + inline error. Click to reset to idle and retry.
accessibility_considerations: Button aria-label changes per state explaining the required action ('Hold to send' / 'Authenticate to send' / 'Sent' / 'Couldn't send'). Press-and-hold has a keyboard alternative: holding Enter for the same duration triggers the same fire. Reduced-motion: progress bar still fills (it's information, not decoration) but state morph becomes instant color swap. Color is supplemented by icons (check/X) and text labels. Failure state's inline error button is keyboard-reachable. Biometric path uses WebAuthn — standard a11y supported.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Friction calibrated to amount (1.5s hold for <$50, 2.5s for <$1000, biometric for >$1000) — trust theater scales with stakes. 2) Button MORPHS in place between idle/sending/sent/failed — no modal, no page nav, the user's attention is anchored. 3) Receipt link slides in 4s after success (non-blocking) — closure without interrupting the next action.

title: Geo choropleth with state-level + drill-to-county and population-normalized comparisons
design_brief: You're building an election results / public-data map (think NYT's election needle, FiveThirtyEight's maps, the COVID dashboards from JHU done right). Pain point: most choropleth maps either show absolute counts (huge states dominate) or just percentage (loses magnitude), and the drill from state→county feels like a different page. The bar: NYT election graphics, The Pudding's data maps, Datawrapper's choropleth.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Single hue for everything — can't tell direction of metric
Tooltip that just repeats the visible color/label
Modal popup with details on state click
Bivariate color scale with 9 colors (unreadable)
Animating state polygons on hover (jitter)
Map that scrolls with the page (loses orientation)
ux_reasoning:
A choropleth must answer three questions: which areas are extreme, which areas tell a different story when normalized, and what's INSIDE an interesting area. Standard choropleths only answer the first.

Color: a diverging OKLCH scale centered on a meaningful midpoint (e.g., 50% for vote share). Negative direction: oklch(0.55 0.18 250) (blue). Positive direction: oklch(0.55 0.18 25) (red). Neutral midpoint: oklch(0.95 0.005 250). This is the bipolar scale used for political/financial data — colorblind-tested (the OKLCH lightness ramp is monotonic on both sides).

Normalization toggle: at the top, a segmented control switches the metric between 'Count', 'Per capita', and 'Per square mile'. Switching reflows the color encoding over 300ms with a FLIP-style cross-fade per polygon. This is the killer move: the SAME geography shows fundamentally different stories at different normalizations, and the user controls the lens.

Drill: click a state → map zooms (camera transform, NOT a new page) to fit that state's bounding box over 600ms with ease-out, and the data resolution upgrades to county-level for that state. Other states fade to 30% opacity and become unclickable (focus mode). Press Esc or click outside: zooms back out.

Hover behavior: tooltip shows ONLY the comparative info ('AL: 52% — 3 pp above national avg, 1 pp below 2020'). Never repeats the visible color or the state name (the user just hovered, they know what it is). This is the heatmap-tooltip discipline applied to maps.

Legend: an interactive legend bar at the bottom shows the color scale with the current data distribution as a faint histogram overlaid. Dragging on the legend selects a value range; states outside the range dim. This is the Tableau legend filter, perfected.

Projection: Albers USA (proper composite that handles Alaska/Hawaii) for US data, Equal Earth for world. Never Mercator for thematic maps — distorts area, distorts perception.

Motion: state polygon hover is instant (no fill transition). Drill-zoom is 600ms ease-out. Normalization change is a 300ms color crossfade per polygon (we can do this because we use CSS variable interpolation, not SVG fill attribute changes).

Rejected: a 3D extruded version where height encodes magnitude. Looks impressive once; impossible to compare adjacent values. Also rejected: bivariate color scales for showing two metrics simultaneously. They're famous but unreadable for most users; better to switch metrics than overlay them.

design_tokens.colors: Diverging scale: oklch(0.55 0.18 250) ↔ oklch(0.95 0.005 250) ↔ oklch(0.55 0.18 25). 7 steps. Drilled-out states dim: 0.3 opacity. Hover state outline: 2px oklch(0.2 0.01 250).
design_tokens.typography: Tooltip: Inter 13px for primary metric, 11px for comparative text. Legend label: 11px tabular-nums. State labels (only at zoomed levels): 11px regular oklch(0.5).
design_tokens.spacing: Map fills container. Legend bar: 24px tall, 320px wide centered at bottom. Normalize toggle: 200px wide, top-right.
design_tokens.motion: Drill-zoom: 600ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). Normalization recolor: 300ms ease-out via CSS var transition. Hover: instant. Other states dim: 200ms ease-out.

component_architecture: <Choropleth<TFeature extends GeoFeature>> generic. Renders SVG polygons with fill via CSS variables. <NormalizeToggle /> + <Legend /> + <Map onSelectState={...} /> children. State data lives in a useChoroplethData(normalization) hook that returns a Map<featureId, value>. Drill state: { focusedStateId, zoomBbox }. Camera transform handled via SVG viewBox interpolation (not d3-zoom — overkill).
interaction_details: Click state polygon: map zooms (viewBox tween 600ms ease-out) to fit that state with 15% padding; other states dim to 30%. Esc or click outside resets.
Hover state: tooltip shows comparative info ('AL: 52.3 · +3.1 pp vs national'), not redundant state name + raw value.
Normalize toggle changes between Count / Per capita / Per square mile: polygons recolor over 300ms via CSS fill transition. Same geography, different story.
Legend bar drag: select a value range; states outside the range dim. Click empty legend to reset.
Keyboard: Tab focuses the map; arrow keys move focus between adjacent states (via geographic adjacency lookup); Enter drills.
accessibility_considerations: role='img' on SVG with aria-label describing the map. Each <path> has aria-label with state name + value (screen readers can navigate states via tabbing). A parallel <table> (visually hidden) provides full data sortable by SR users — pure-visual maps are inaccessible without this. Keyboard nav between states uses an adjacency graph. Reduced-motion: viewBox tween becomes instant; recolor transition becomes instant. Color: diverging scale is colorblind-tested (OKLCH monotonic lightness on each side); values supplemented by hover tooltip with numeric comparisons.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Normalization toggle (Count / Per capita / Per area) is a first-class control — same map tells different stories, user chooses the lens. 2) Drill-to-state is a viewBox zoom with focus dimming, not a page navigation — the user never loses geographic orientation. 3) Tooltips show comparative analysis ('+3.1 pp vs national'), not the raw value the color already encodes.

title: Progressive disclosure setup wizard that lives inside the app, not over it
design_brief: You're building the first-run setup for a code-collaboration tool (think Linear's workspace setup, Vercel's team onboarding, Sentry's project setup). Pain point: most apps either show a 5-step modal at first login OR drop the user in a half-empty app and have them figure it out. The bar: Linear's setup (sidebar reveals new sections as they're set up), Vercel's project initialization, Sentry's wizard with platform-specific deep linking.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Modal overlay 'Welcome! Step 1 of 5'
Progress checklist as a separate page
Sample data the user can't delete easily
Setup steps that disable the rest of the app until complete
Required fields that are actually optional (name your team, pick a color)
Setup completion congratulations animation
ux_reasoning:
Setup IS the app. The user should be in the app from second one, with the setup tasks visible as ambient progress in the sidebar, not as a stopping point.

Layout: standard app sidebar with sections (Issues, Projects, Members, Settings). But at first login, only Issues + Settings are visible. As the user completes setup steps (invite a teammate, create a project, configure a calendar), new sections REVEAL in the sidebar with a brief 200ms slide-in. The sidebar IS the progress bar — concrete and discoverable.

In the main view, when the user is in a section that has no content yet, the empty state is a 'first-time' panel that's interactive — like the sample-workspace from batch 01 (task 18) but for actions: 'Create your first project' as a fully styled mock project card that becomes real when the user clicks any field on it. The mock IS the form.

Progress visibility: a tiny chevron in the sidebar header expands a 'Setup' tray (40px tall) showing 'Invite teammate · Create project · Connect Slack' as inline links. Click any to deep-link into that flow. Click chevron again to collapse. After all setup tasks are done, the chevron + tray disappear permanently. NO 'You've completed setup!' celebration.

Deferred deep-link: setup tasks are not required. The user can ignore the Setup tray and use the app. If they spend 5+ sessions without inviting a teammate, the tray subtly highlights the 'Invite teammate' task on next session (no notification, no badge — just a single 1px underline pulse for 4s). This is the 'recommendation without nagging' principle.

Deep-link state: if the user clicks 'Connect Slack' from the tray, we deep-link to that exact setting + open the Slack auth flow. After auth completes, we navigate them back to where they were AND reveal the new sidebar section ('Slack notifications').

Rejected: gamification (badges, points, streaks) for setup completion. Hostile to professional software users; treats adults like Duolingo learners. Also rejected: AI assistant prompting setup. Adds chrome without solving the underlying problem (the app should teach itself).

design_tokens.colors: Sidebar section reveal: 200ms slide-in. Setup tray: oklch(0.96 0.005 250) bg, single divider above. Pulsing highlight on neglected task: oklch(0.55 0.18 250) underline at 4s decay.
design_tokens.typography: Sidebar section titles: Inter 13px medium. Setup tray items: 12px regular. Mock-as-form placeholder text: italic 13px oklch(0.55).
design_tokens.spacing: Sidebar width: 240px. Section padding: 8px. Setup tray height: 40px collapsed (chevron only), 120px expanded.
design_tokens.motion: Section reveal: 200ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1) translateX(-8px → 0) + opacity. Tray expand: 200ms ease-out height. Neglected-task pulse: 4000ms linear underline-opacity 0 → 1 → 0.

component_architecture: <SetupContext /> tracks completed setup steps; <Sidebar /> reads context to decide which sections to render. <SetupTray /> renders inline links for incomplete tasks. <MockAsForm /> components are used for empty states — they accept a setup step ID and call onComplete when the user fills in the first real value, dispatching to setup context.
interaction_details: First login: sidebar shows only Issues + Settings. As setup tasks complete, new sections slide in (200ms).
Empty 'Projects' page: shows a MockProjectAsForm card that LOOKS like a project (status dot, name slot). Typing a name + Enter creates the real project AND reveals 'Projects' in the sidebar.
Click chevron in sidebar bottom: expands a Setup tray (40px → 120px) listing incomplete steps. Click any to deep-link.
If user hasn't completed a task after 5 sessions: that task's label gets a 4s underline pulse next session — subtle reminder without notification.
All setup complete: Setup tray + chevron disappear permanently. No celebration.
accessibility_considerations: Sidebar is a real <nav> with descriptive aria-label. New sections sliding in are announced via aria-live='polite' ('Projects section now available'). Setup tray expansion uses aria-expanded. MockAsForm has proper <label htmlFor> on inputs. Pulsing reminder uses CSS animation only — no aria-live (would be intrusive); the visual reminder is supplemented by the persistent tray. Reduced-motion: section reveal slide becomes instant; pulse underline becomes immediate solid. Color: completion state isn't color-only — sections appear vs don't appear, tasks listed vs hidden.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Sidebar IS the progress bar — sections reveal as setup completes, no separate progress UI. 2) Empty states are interactive mocks that become real on first input — no modal wizard, just typing into placeholders. 3) Neglected tasks get a 4s underline pulse after 5 sessions — recommendation without nagging, never a notification badge.

title: Address autocomplete with country-specific format inference and inline validation
design_brief: You're building the shipping address form for a global e-commerce checkout (think Stripe Checkout, Shopify Shop Pay, Amazon's address form). Pain point: most address forms are 8 separate fields in US format, breaking for international users (UK has postcode + town, Japan reads right-to-left, Brazil needs CEP-based autofill). The bar: Stripe Checkout's address autocomplete, Shopify's adaptive forms, Apple Pay's address sheet.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Country dropdown that doesn't change other field labels
Fixed 'State/Province/Region' field even for countries without states
Postcode validation that's a regex for US ZIPs
Manual entry as primary path — autocomplete should be primary
Address line 1 + 2 + city + state + zip + country as 6 separate inputs
Generic 'Enter a valid address' error
ux_reasoning:
Addresses are wildly different across countries (locale-specific). Naively forcing 'Street, City, State, ZIP' on a Japanese user generates wrong data. The form must ADAPT to the country, ideally before the user even types.

Primary input: a single combobox 'Search address' at the top. Typing fetches Google Places (or similar) suggestions. Selecting one populates the rest of the form. This is the Stripe / Shopify pattern: most users type 3-5 characters, pick a result, done.

Manual entry as escape hatch: a 'Enter manually' link below the combobox. Click → fields appear, adapted to the SELECTED country (or detected IP country). For US: address1, address2, city, state, zip. For UK: address1, address2, town, county (optional), postcode. For JP: postcode (first!), prefecture, city, street, number, building (postcode in JP populates the rest via JIS lookup). For DE: street + number on one line (street + house number split), postcode, city.

Country change reflows fields: when the user changes country (combobox at top), fields below RE-RENDER smoothly with FLIP-style choreography over 300ms. Field labels change ('ZIP' → 'Postcode'), field order changes (JP puts postcode first), validation regex changes. This is the killer interaction: same form, ten countries' worth of structures.

Field-level validation: validates on blur. For postcodes: country-specific regex + an async lookup for valid postal areas. For UK postcodes: hyphen optional, space optional, case-insensitive, but reformatted on blur to canonical form ('sw1a 1aa' → 'SW1A 1AA'). For JP postcodes: 7 digits, optional hyphen after 3 — reformatted to '###-####'.

Autocomplete keyboard nav: type, arrow keys move through suggestions, Enter selects, Esc closes. Selected suggestion populates instantly; user sees the form fill in. This sub-second feedback is what makes the address form not feel like a form.

Rejected: a 'find on map' modal as primary input. Adds friction for a fast-typed input. Map view is a tertiary option.

design_tokens.colors: Input border: oklch(0.85 0.005 250) idle, oklch(0.55 0.18 250) focus, oklch(0.6 0.18 25) error, oklch(0.6 0.14 145) valid. Autocomplete dropdown: white bg with 1px border, 4-row visible. Country flag chip: tiny emoji (yes, here flags are fine — they're identity, not status).
design_tokens.typography: Labels: Inter 13px medium. Inputs: 14px regular. Autocomplete suggestions: 14px regular with bold emphasis on matched substring.
design_tokens.spacing: Input height: 36px. Field gap: 12px. Autocomplete dropdown row: 40px. Field group (e.g., city + state + zip) sits on one row when space permits.
design_tokens.motion: Field reflow on country change: 300ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1) FLIP (fields keep refs, animate via translation). Validation state transitions: 150ms ease-out border-color. Autocomplete fade: 100ms ease-out.

component_architecture: <AddressForm<TCountry>> generic. Address schema is country-keyed: addressSchemas[countryCode] returns ordered field defs. <CountrySelect /> + <AddressAutocomplete /> + <FieldSet /> (rendered per country's schema). useFieldFlip() hook captures field bbox before country change and animates after.
interaction_details: Country change: fields below FLIP-reflow over 300ms — labels translate, new fields fade in, removed fields fade out, same field IDs keep their values.
Postcode blur: reformats to canonical (UK: 'sw1a1aa' → 'SW1A 1AA'; JP: '1234567' → '123-4567'). Visual feedback is immediate.
Invalid postcode: border turns red, inline error appears below with country-specific message ('Enter a 5-digit ZIP' / 'Enter a valid UK postcode').
Field tab order: matches schema order per country (so JP users tab postcode → prefecture → city, US users tab address1 → address2 → city → state → zip).
Switching country preserves matching fields ('city' value stays when switching US→GB), drops country-unique fields ('state' lost when switching to UK).
accessibility_considerations: Every field is a real <label htmlFor> + <input>. aria-invalid + aria-describedby on errors. Country select is a real <select> — uses native OS picker (much faster than custom dropdowns for SR users). Error messages use role='alert'. RTL languages (Arabic, Hebrew) get dir='rtl' on the form root when country implies them. Reduced-motion: FLIP reflow becomes instant. Color: validation state is supplemented by inline text and aria-invalid; never color-only.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Country-keyed schema with FLIP-choreographed field reflow on country change — the same form is 10 different forms depending on locale. 2) Postcode formatting on blur normalizes input ('sw1a1aa' → 'SW1A 1AA') — respects how users actually type. 3) Field IDs are stable across schemas where possible ('city' is the same in US/GB/DE) so values persist when switching countries.

title: 3D site planner with grid-snapped object placement and elevation toggle
design_brief: You're building a simplified architectural site planner (think a web version of Twinmotion + SketchUp's web app + the planner mode in The Sims 4). Users place buildings, trees, paths on a 3D plot. Pain point: most 3D placement tools either require pro CAD knowledge OR are 2D-only (loses the spatial intuition the 3D affords). The bar: SketchUp Web's drag-to-place, Twinmotion's library, The Sims' build mode.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Free-rotation orbit camera with no snap angles (users get lost)
Click-to-place without preview ghosting
Generic gray placeholder geometry for all objects
Sun/shadow that's static and doesn't update with elevation
Object rotation by typing degrees in a sidebar
Floating 'Save' button while in 3D — should be ambient
ux_reasoning:
3D placement tools fail in two ways: too much freedom (users lose orientation) or too little (lose spatial intuition). The right model is constrained freedom: free camera, but grid-snapped placement and snap-rotation angles.

Camera: orbit camera with snap-to-axis on release. Drag rotates freely; on mouse-up, camera lerps to the nearest 45° angle over 300ms. This means views are always 'aligned' for placement clarity. Shift+drag bypasses snap for fine adjustment.

Elevation toggle: a vertical slider on the right (think height adjustment in The Sims) controls camera elevation from 'plan' (top-down) to 'eye' (1.7m). Mid-positions interpolate. This is the killer move: users can swap between architectural planning view and walkthrough perspective without leaving the scene.

Object library: a horizontal strip at the bottom with 12 object categories (buildings, trees, paths, fences, water, ...). Each shows a 3D-rendered thumbnail. Click an object → drag a 'ghost' across the scene → release to place. Ghost is the actual object at 50% opacity, snapped to a 1m grid. Rotation while holding: scroll wheel snaps to 15° increments.

Sun & shadow: a small dial at top-left controls time of day (6am → 6pm). Sun position + shadow casts update in real-time as user drags the dial. This isn't decorative — architects use shadow studies to plan placement. We use a directional light + shadow map; shadows are PCF-filtered for soft edges.

Object manipulation after placement: click selects (highlight + transform gizmo). Drag the gizmo arrows to translate (snapped to grid). Scroll on selected object to rotate. Delete key removes. Esc deselects. Cmd+Z undoes — every action is in an undo queue (max 50).

Performance: instanced meshes for repeated objects (trees, fences) — single draw call for hundreds of identical objects. Frameloop='demand'; redraw only on interaction.

Rejected: AR/VR mode. Beautiful but the target user is a homeowner or small architect — they want quick desktop interaction. AR is a feature flag, not a default. Also rejected: photorealistic rendering. The aesthetic should be 'designed', not 'simulated' — a stylized look ages better and stays performant.

design_tokens.colors: Ground: oklch(0.86 0.04 100) (light grass green-yellow). Grid lines: oklch(0.8 0.03 100) at 0.5px. Sky: vertical gradient oklch(0.85 0.05 230) (top) → oklch(0.95 0.04 60) (horizon). Object highlight: oklch(0.55 0.18 250) 2px outline. Ghost: 50% opacity overlay of selected object's color.
design_tokens.typography: Object names on hover: Inter 12px on dark pill. Time-of-day dial label: 11px tabular-nums.
design_tokens.spacing: Object library strip: 80px tall, full width. Elevation slider: 8px wide, 240px tall. Sun dial: 64px diameter.
design_tokens.motion: Camera snap-to-angle on release: 300ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). Object place: instant (no spring). Sun position with shadow: real-time on dial drag. Ghost follow: 60Hz.

component_architecture: <SitePlanner /> with <Canvas /> root. Children: <SceneObjects />, <PlacementGhost />, <SunSystem timeOfDay={hour} />, <SelectionGizmo />. State: scene objects array (each { id, kind, position, rotation }), selectedId, ghost (kind + position during placement). useGridSnap() rounds positions to 1m. useUndoStack() tracks scene mutations.
interaction_details: Click library item: enters placement mode. Ghost (50% opacity) follows cursor, snapped to 1m grid. Click ground: places object. Esc cancels.
Camera orbit: drag rotates. On mouse-up: lerps to nearest 45° over 300ms. Shift+drag bypasses snap.
Elevation slider (right rail): top = plan view (camera looks straight down), bottom = eye-level walkthrough. Smooth interpolation.
Sun dial (top-left): drag to change time of day; shadows recompute in real-time, sun direction updates the directional light.
Select placed object: highlight + transform gizmo. Scroll on selected object: rotates by 15° per tick.
accessibility_considerations: Canvas has aria-label describing the planner. A parallel <ul> lists all placed objects (visually hidden, SR-accessible) — pure-visual 3D is inaccessible without this. Library buttons are real buttons with aria-pressed for active mode. Range inputs are native <input type='range'> with aria-label. Keyboard: arrow keys move selected object by 1m grid units; Delete removes; Cmd+Z undoes. Reduced-motion: camera snap on release becomes instant; ghost still follows but without smoothing. Time-of-day dial values announce via aria-valuetext ('2 PM').
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Camera snap-to-45° on release means views are always 'aligned' for planning clarity — Shift+drag for fine control when needed. 2) Vertical elevation slider transitions camera from plan view to eye-level — same scene, two mental models. 3) Live sun position dial with PCF-soft shadows lets users do real shadow studies, not just decorative lighting.

title: Note-taking app with thumb-zone gestures on mobile and bi-pane editor on desktop
design_brief: You're building a note-taking app (think Bear, Craft, Obsidian Mobile, Apple Notes). The same notes must be writable in 3 seconds on a phone in a bus AND in 30-minute deep-work sessions on a desktop with two notes open. Pain point: most cross-device note apps shrink desktop UI to mobile and call it responsive. The bar: Bear (the iOS thumb-zone master), Craft's desktop editing, Obsidian's pane management.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Floating + button at bottom-right on mobile (out of thumb reach for left-handers, hidden by browser bar)
Same toolbar layout on phone and laptop
Sidebar nav as a hamburger that opens a left drawer (slow + thumb unfriendly)
Desktop with only single-pane editing
Keyboard shortcut hints on mobile
Mobile share sheet that's a custom in-app drawer instead of OS share
ux_reasoning:
Mobile note creation should be quick capture. Desktop note editing should be deep work. These are different mental models requiring different UI.

Mobile: bottom-anchored navigation in thumb zone (44px tall bar at bottom with 4 icons: notes, search, new, settings). New note is a single tap and the editor opens with the title field focused — never a modal. Swipe-right on a note in the list archives; swipe-left deletes (with 5s undo). Long-press on a note: contextual menu RISES from the bottom (sheet style, thumb-friendly), NEVER from the row top. The 'plus' for formatting toolbar slides up from above the keyboard when the user is typing, so it's right where the thumbs already are.

Desktop: standard sidebar (notes list) + main editor. Pressing Cmd+\ splits the editor into two panes — left shows current note, right is empty for a second note to be opened. This is the killer feature: side-by-side note editing for research, comparing, copying between notes. The split has a draggable divider. Pressing Cmd+\ again closes the right pane.

Note list on mobile: cards with title + first-line preview + relative timestamp. On desktop: dense rows with title + preview + tags + date columns. Same data, different density.

Markdown editing: WYSIWYG on both. On mobile, formatting bar above keyboard. On desktop, formatting via keyboard shortcuts (Cmd+B for bold, Cmd+I for italic, etc.) — toolbar exists in a hover-revealed menu bar to teach the shortcuts.

Sync indicator: when offline-edited notes are syncing, a small icon appears next to the title. NEVER a modal 'Syncing...' overlay. Failure to sync: title gets a subtle dot + tooltip 'Saved locally, will sync when online'. Never a banner.

Rejected: a 'simple mode' / 'advanced mode' switch. Real users use the same app on both devices; toggle mode would confuse. Two interaction philosophies, one app, modality-detected. Also rejected: voice input as a first-class entry point on mobile. Native dictation already exists in the OS keyboard.

design_tokens.colors: App bg: oklch(0.99 0.005 60) (warm white, easier on eyes for long reading). Sidebar: oklch(0.97 0.005 60). Sync indicator: subtle pulse oklch(0.6 0.18 250). Note row hover (desktop): oklch(0.96 0.005 60).
design_tokens.typography: Note title: Inter 17px medium (mobile), 16px medium (desktop). Body: Inter 16px / 24px (mobile), 15px / 24px (desktop). Date: 12px tabular-nums.
design_tokens.spacing: Mobile bottom bar: 44px tall + safe-area inset. Note card on mobile: 80px tall with padding. Desktop row: 48px tall.
design_tokens.motion: Bottom sheet on mobile: 350ms cubic-bezier(0.32, 0.72, 0, 1). Pane split on desktop: 200ms ease-out width transition. Sync pulse: 1500ms ease-in-out infinite.

component_architecture: <NoteApp /> reads modality from useInputModality(). MobileNoteApp + DesktopNoteApp share a NotesContext (the data layer). MobileNoteApp uses <BottomNav /> + <NotesList />, DesktopNoteApp uses <Sidebar /> + <EditorPanes />. Pane management is desktop-only state.
interaction_details: Mobile: tap 'New' in bottom nav → editor opens with focus on body. No modal, no dialog.
Mobile: swipe right on note in list → archives with 5s undo. Swipe left → delete with 5s undo.
Mobile: long-press a note → bottom sheet rises with contextual actions.
Desktop: Cmd+\ splits the editor into two panes. Same key closes the right pane. Drag divider to resize.
Both: sync indicator (tiny pulsing dot) on title when offline edits are pending. Never a banner.
accessibility_considerations: All interactive elements are real buttons with aria-label. Bottom nav uses real <nav> + <button>. Reduced-motion: bottom sheet animation becomes instant; pane split becomes instant. Mobile thumb-zone navigation is reachable from VoiceOver rotor + standard swipe gestures. Keyboard nav on desktop: Cmd+\ split, Cmd+1/2 focus left/right pane, Cmd+N new note. Color: sync status supplemented by aria-label.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Cmd+\ split on desktop opens a second pane for side-by-side note editing — the killer feature for research workflows. 2) Mobile bottom nav lives in the thumb zone with safe-area-inset padding — usable one-handed in motion. 3) Contextual actions on mobile rise from the bottom (sheet style), never from the row's top — always in thumb reach.

title: Accessible carousel with rotor-friendly slide model and pause-on-focus
design_brief: You're building the hero carousel for a marketing site (think Apple's product showcase, Stripe's home page, Linear's marketing). Carousels are notoriously bad for accessibility (motion + auto-advance + hidden content). Pain point: most carousels fail WCAG 2.2.2 (pause/stop/hide) and 2.1.1 (keyboard accessible). The bar: WAI-ARIA APG carousel pattern, Inclusive Components by Heydon Pickering, Apple's marketing carousels.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Auto-rotating slides that can't be paused
Carousel where keyboard focus moves to off-screen slides
Dots-only navigation that's a single tab stop with no slide preview
Animation that ignores prefers-reduced-motion
Carousel with no aria-label or unclear name
Pagination dots in light gray on white that fail contrast
ux_reasoning:
The carousel is one of the most-misimplemented patterns in web UI. The WAI-ARIA APG spec is precise; we follow it.

Structure: a region with role='region' aria-roledescription='carousel' aria-label='Featured products'. Inside, a 'slides' container with each slide as role='group' aria-roledescription='slide' aria-label='Slide N of M' aria-hidden bound to current state. Controls: previous, next, slide pickers (proper buttons, not divs).

Auto-advance behavior: if auto-advance is enabled (5s per slide), a pause button is REQUIRED (WCAG 2.2.2). Hovering OR keyboard-focusing the carousel automatically pauses; un-hovering/blurring resumes. This is the polite default. User can also manually pause permanently via the Pause button.

Reduced-motion: auto-advance disables entirely. Slide transitions become instant (no slide animation). Users get a static carousel they navigate manually.

Keyboard nav: Tab into carousel, Tab to navigate between controls (prev, slide indicators, next, pause). Arrow keys on the slide area: left/right moves slides. Home/End: first/last slide. Slides themselves should NOT be in the tab order when off-screen (aria-hidden + inert).

Slide indicators: tablist pattern. Each indicator is a tab; the slide is the tabpanel. This is more semantic than dots-as-buttons. Selected tab is aria-selected.

Motion: slide transition is a translateX over 400ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). No fade, no scale, no parallax — slide MEANS slide. Reduced-motion: zero transition, content swaps instantly.

The interaction must work without JavaScript (progressive enhancement): a CSS scroll-snap version is the no-JS fallback, where each slide is a scroll-snap anchor in a horizontally-scrolling container.

Rejected: a 3D 'coverflow' carousel. Bad for a11y, slow, hard to navigate. Also rejected: pause indicator that's a small icon — the pause button is a real button with clear text and icon.

design_tokens.colors: Indicator inactive: oklch(0.6 0.005 250) at 40% opacity. Indicator active: oklch(0.2 0.01 250). Prev/next buttons: oklch(0.18 0.005 250 / 0.7) bg with white text (3:1 contrast minimum). Focus ring: 2px outline oklch(0.55 0.18 250).
design_tokens.typography: Slide content: any. Slide aria-label: 'Slide 2 of 5'. Controls: 14px medium when text-labeled.
design_tokens.spacing: Slide indicators row: 8px gap, 24px from carousel bottom. Prev/next: 48px square (touch target). Pause button: 36px square in top-right corner.
design_tokens.motion: Slide transition: 400ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1) translateX. Reduced-motion: 0ms. Indicator change: instant.

component_architecture: <Carousel<TSlide>> generic over slide data. <CarouselSlide /> renders one slide with proper ARIA. <CarouselIndicators /> renders the tablist. <CarouselControls /> renders prev/next/pause buttons. usePolitePause() pauses auto-advance on hover/focus; reduces to off on reduced-motion.
interaction_details: Auto-advance: slides advance every 5s. Hovering or keyboard-focusing the carousel pauses politely; un-hovering/blurring resumes.
Pause button (top-right): user-controlled permanent pause. Resumes auto-advance when un-paused.
Keyboard: arrow left/right moves slides; Home/End jumps to first/last; tab cycles through controls.
Reduced-motion: auto-advance disabled entirely; slide transitions become 0ms (instant content swap).
Off-screen slides are aria-hidden + inert — keyboard focus never enters them, screen readers don't announce them.
accessibility_considerations: role='region' aria-roledescription='carousel' with descriptive aria-label. Slides are role='group' aria-roledescription='slide' with 'N of M' label. Off-screen slides are aria-hidden + inert (focus + interaction excluded). Indicators use the tablist/tab/tabpanel pattern with aria-controls + aria-selected. Pause button visible and labeled (WCAG 2.2.2). Polite pause on hover/focus. Reduced-motion completely disables auto-advance and animations. Focus ring is 2px outline (not box-shadow). Contrast: all control text/icons at 4.5:1 on the dimmed background.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Polite pause on hover OR keyboard focus — auto-advance never interrupts a reader. Manual pause button for permanent control (WCAG 2.2.2 compliant). 2) Off-screen slides use both aria-hidden AND inert — modern way to exclude focus and announcement without breaking the layout. 3) Reduced-motion fully disables auto-advance (not just animation removal) — respects user intent.

title: Search results with predictive prefetch and stale-while-revalidate ranking
design_brief: You're building search results for a documentation site (think Algolia DocSearch, Stripe docs search, the Linear search). Pain point: most docs search either feels instant but shows stale results, or shows fresh results but with a noticeable 200ms delay per keystroke. The bar: Linear's command palette search, Stripe's docs search, Algolia DocSearch's classic UI.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Spinner inside search input on every keystroke
Search button required to commit query
Server search on every keystroke without debouncing
Cached results displayed alongside loading spinner with no visual distinction
Suggestions dropdown that takes 600ms to first render
'No results' that don't preserve previous results
ux_reasoning:
Search perception lives in the ~100ms after a keystroke. Three layers stacked:

Layer 1 — local cache: for the last 50 queries, results are kept in an LRU cache. Typing a previously-typed query: results from cache appear in <16ms. The cache is keyed by exact query, normalized (lowercase, trimmed). Cache entries have a maxAge of 5 minutes.

Layer 2 — local fuzzy index: a tiny Lunr or MiniSearch index of the most popular docs (top 200 pages, ~200KB precomputed and bundled). Any query that's not in cache: runs against the local index in <30ms. Results appear immediately, marked subtle (1px dotted border under each result indicates 'local' provenance).

Layer 3 — server search: in parallel with the local search, an XHR fires (debounced 150ms — no debounce on the local layers). When server results arrive, they REPLACE the local results in place, with a 200ms crossfade. The dotted-border marker disappears as server-authoritative results appear.

Stale-while-revalidate: if a cached query result is shown, but more than 30s has elapsed since the cache was populated, we also fire a background server query and update the results in place. The user sees instant + accurate.

Predictive prefetch: as the user types each character, we prefetch the FIRST result's actual page content (HTML) in the background. By the time they press Enter to open, the page is in browser cache. Critical: only prefetch the top result (not all visible), to limit bandwidth.

No results: when both layers return zero, we show 'No matches for "X"' but preserve the LAST non-empty result set below with 'Showing previous results' — never clear the screen. This prevents the flash of empty + the loss of context.

Motion: result crossfade is 200ms opacity only. NO scale, NO slide, NO stagger. Search is utilitarian; speed is the value, not choreography.

Rejected: a 'natural language' AI search as the default. Slow (1-3s latency), expensive, often wrong for docs. Real search with great UX is the right primary path; AI is a fallback button.

design_tokens.colors: Result hover: oklch(0.97 0.005 250). Result active (keyboard focus): oklch(0.94 0.04 250). Local-source indicator: 1px dotted oklch(0.55 0.005 250) at result bottom. Query match highlight: oklch(0.95 0.1 60) bg on matched substring.
design_tokens.typography: Result title: Inter 14px medium. Path breadcrumb: 12px regular oklch(0.55). Excerpt: 13px regular with matched substrings highlighted.
design_tokens.spacing: Search input: 44px tall. Result row: 56px. Dropdown max-height: 480px. Dropdown width: matches input.
design_tokens.motion: Result crossfade (local → server): 200ms opacity. No other animations. Keyboard navigation: instant focus change.

component_architecture: <DocsSearch /> root with <SearchInput /> + <ResultsDropdown />. useLayeredSearch(query) hook returns { results, source: 'cache' | 'local' | 'server', isStale }. Uses an LRU cache instance + a MiniSearch instance + a server fetch (debounced 150ms via custom useDebouncedFetch). Predictive prefetch happens via <link rel='prefetch'> appended to head.
interaction_details: Type a query: local index returns results in <30ms with a 1px dotted underline marker. Server query fires in background (debounced 150ms).
Server response arrives: results replace local with 200ms opacity crossfade; dotted underline disappears.
Repeat a query within 30s: cached results in <16ms; no server fetch.
Repeat a query after 30s: cached results shown instantly, server query fires for revalidation, results update silently.
No matches: 'No results' message at top, but PREVIOUS results stay visible below with 'showing previous' subtle line.
accessibility_considerations: role='listbox' / role='option' / aria-selected for results. aria-activedescendant ties input focus to focused result. Source indicator (dotted underline) is decorative (no a11y impact). 'Showing previous results' is announced via the aria-live region (status). Predictive prefetch is via <link rel='prefetch'> — invisible to a11y tree. Reduced-motion: crossfade becomes instant. Color: focused row has bg shift; result source is supplemented by no announcement (it's an implementation detail).
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Three-layer search (cache → local → server) with stale-while-revalidate — instant results that converge to authoritative without spinners. 2) Predictive prefetch via <link rel='prefetch'> on the top result — Enter feels instant because the page is already in browser cache. 3) 'No matches' preserves the previous result set below — the screen never flashes empty.

title: Neo-skeumorphic camera control UI with engraved labels and metal-knob interactions
design_brief: You're building the controls panel for a web-based camera/microphone app (think Capture One's tool palettes, the new Apple Final Cut, audio plugin UIs). Pain point: 'neumorphism' from 2020 was flat shadows, not real skeuomorphism. The bar: pro audio plugin UIs (UAD, Waves), Capture One's color editor, real-world hardware (Leica controls, Korg synths).
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Neumorphism with double-shadow on flat colored rectangles
Bouncy knob rotation
Generic dial that snaps to integers without inertia
Tooltip on every knob explaining what it does
Knob labels in the same font as body UI
Click-and-drag knob that's hard to do with a trackpad
ux_reasoning:
Skeuomorphism done right is about MATERIALITY (how surfaces catch light) and AFFORDANCE (what the control suggests doing), not iconography. Audio plugin UIs are the canonical reference — they were never replaced by flat design because pros prefer the tactile model.

Knob design: a circular brushed-metal knob with a single tick mark indicating value position. The brushed-metal texture is a radial-gradient CSS approximation (white + gray bands every 4°). Beveled edge: inset shadow on top-left (light) + outset shadow on bottom-right (dark). The tick mark is a 2px line from edge to center. Rotation maps to value via SVG transform.

Interaction: click-and-drag VERTICALLY on a knob changes value (up = increase, down = decrease). This is the audio-plugin convention, NOT click-and-rotate (which is wrist-painful and trackpad-impossible). Vertical-pixel-to-value sensitivity: 100 pixels = full range. Shift+drag = fine adjustment (10x slower).

Double-click resets to default. Right-click opens a small typed-input popover ('Enter exact value').

Labels: engraved into the panel using a CSS technique — text with 1px white text-shadow (highlight) + 1px dark text-shadow (depression). Looks etched. Font: small caps Inter at 10px with 0.1em letter-spacing. NO icons — labels are words.

Panel material: a brushed aluminum gradient (multi-stop linear-gradient like the Y2K chrome from batch 01 but with finer bands). Rivets at corners (small circular gradients with inset shadow). Screws are functional-looking but non-interactive.

Level meters: real-time audio level visualization as horizontal LED strips. Each LED is a 4px wide colored rect — green for safe, yellow for hot, red for clipping. Lit LEDs are full color; unlit LEDs are dim. Peak hold: a single bright LED stays lit for 1.5s at the peak position before decaying.

Motion: knob rotation is instant (1:1 with drag). Tick mark transition: never animated. Level meter: 60Hz update via raf. Peak decay: linear over 1500ms.

Rejected: photorealistic 3D knobs with global illumination. The CSS approximation is GOOD ENOUGH and stays performant. Real audio plugin UIs use 2D rendered images, not 3D. Also rejected: a 'flat mode' toggle for users who don't like skeuomorphism. The whole point is conviction.

design_tokens.colors: Panel: linear-gradient(160deg, oklch(0.85 0.005 250), oklch(0.75 0.005 250), oklch(0.7 0.005 250)). Knob base: radial-gradient(oklch(0.92 0.005 250), oklch(0.75 0.005 250), oklch(0.6 0.005 250)). Knob tick: oklch(0.2 0.01 250). LED green: oklch(0.65 0.16 145). LED yellow: oklch(0.78 0.16 80). LED red: oklch(0.6 0.18 25).
design_tokens.typography: Engraved labels: Inter 10px small-caps, letter-spacing 0.1em. Value display: 'JetBrains Mono' 11px tabular-nums.
design_tokens.spacing: Knob: 56px diameter. Panel padding: 16px. LED strip: 4px gap between LEDs, 4px LED width.
design_tokens.motion: Knob rotation: instant on drag. Peak LED decay: 1500ms linear. Level meter updates: 60Hz.

component_architecture: <ControlPanel /> wraps everything in the brushed-metal panel. <Knob value onChange min max defaultValue label /> is the primitive. <LevelMeter levels peakHold /> renders the LED strip. useDragKnob() hook handles the vertical-drag interaction with sensitivity options.
interaction_details: Drag knob vertically: up increases, down decreases. 100 pixels = full range. Shift+drag = 10x slower (fine).
Double-click knob: resets to default value.
Right-click knob: small popover for typed exact value entry.
Level meter: real-time LED strip; peak hold (a single bright LED stays at peak position for 1.5s then decays linearly).
Engraved labels: small-caps with dual text-shadow (light + dark) for the etched-into-metal look — no underline, no hover state.
accessibility_considerations: Knob uses role='slider' with aria-valuenow / aria-valuemin / aria-valuemax / aria-valuetext (formatted value). Keyboard: arrow up/down adjusts by 1; Shift+arrow by 10; Home/End jump to min/max; PageUp/Down by 10. Level meter has aria-label with current percentage. Reduced-motion: peak-hold animation still works (it's information) but transitions are kept instant. Color: LED colors (green/yellow/red) are supplemented by position (left = safe, right = clipping) — readable without color.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Vertical-drag knob interaction (audio-plugin convention) — wrist-friendly, trackpad-friendly. Shift modifier for fine adjustment. 2) Engraved labels via dual text-shadow (highlight + depression) — looks etched into metal, doesn't try to be a 3D render. 3) Level meter with peak-hold LED that stays for 1.5s then decays — borrowed directly from professional audio hardware.

title: Stripe-style dense subscription metrics dashboard with sparkline-attached KPIs
design_brief: You're building the metrics overview for a SaaS billing dashboard (think Stripe's overview tab, ChartMogul, ProfitWell). PMs and CFOs need to scan 20+ KPIs at once and drill into any. Pain point: most metrics dashboards show 4 huge tile cards with one number each, wasting screen real estate. The bar: Stripe Dashboard's overview, Datadog's metric tiles, June.so's analytics.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: 4-tile grid with 80px-tall numbers and no context
Percentage change shown without a sparkline (no shape, no trend)
Donut chart for every percentage metric
Hover tooltips that show 'Metric: Value' (redundant)
'View report →' link on every tile (adds chrome without value)
Auto-refreshing tiles with no indication that data changed
ux_reasoning:
A metrics dashboard's job is comparative reading at a glance. Each metric needs three things visible simultaneously: the current value, the trend shape (sparkline), and the comparison delta (% vs previous period). Stripe nailed this years ago; most clones still miss it.

Layout: a flexible grid of 'metric strips' instead of cards. Each strip is 56px tall × ~280px wide, containing: metric name (label), current value (big number), delta % vs previous period (with up/down arrow), and an inline sparkline showing the last 30 days. Strips fit 3-4 per row at desktop, 1 per row on mobile.

The sparkline is the load-bearing element. 120px wide × 32px tall, inline next to the number. No axes, no labels, no tooltips — just the shape. The current value is highlighted as a 3px dot at the end. This is the Stripe overview's killer move: trend at a glance, magnitude in the number.

Delta % styling: green if up AND positive metric (revenue), green if down AND negative metric (churn). The system knows which metrics are 'good when up' vs 'good when down'. This is rare in dashboards and crucial — you should never have to think 'is 5% churn UP good or bad?'.

Current period vs comparison: a small chip at the top right of the dashboard ('Last 30 days vs previous 30 days') with a dropdown to change comparison. ALL deltas use this comparison consistently — no metric-by-metric customization.

Click a metric strip: opens a detailed time-series view in a side panel (not a new page). The side panel slides in from the right, dims the dashboard to 60% opacity. Esc closes.

Live updates: when a metric value changes (every 60s polling for most), the new value tweens to the old value over 600ms (a digit-roll effect — only the changed digits animate). The sparkline appends the new point with a 200ms fade-in. NEVER a flash, NEVER a re-render of the whole strip.

Rejected: a 'compare to multiple periods' feature. Adds complexity; one comparison is the right scope for an overview. Power users get the detailed view. Also rejected: customizable strip order via drag-and-drop. Auto-arranged by importance (revenue first, then growth, then churn, then ops); customization is settings panel debt.

design_tokens.colors: Metric value: oklch(0.2 0.01 250). Label: oklch(0.55 0.005 250). Sparkline stroke: oklch(0.55 0.005 250). Sparkline endpoint dot: oklch(0.55 0.18 250). Delta positive: oklch(0.55 0.14 145). Delta negative: oklch(0.6 0.18 25). Strip border: 0.5px oklch(0.93 0.005 250).
design_tokens.typography: Label: Inter 11px uppercase tracking-wider. Value: Inter 22px medium tabular-nums. Delta: Inter 12px tabular-nums. Comparison chip: 12px regular.
design_tokens.spacing: Strip: 56px tall × auto-width. Internal gap: 12px. Sparkline: 120px × 32px right-aligned. Dashboard padding: 16px.
design_tokens.motion: Digit roll on value update: 600ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1) only on changed digits. Sparkline point append: 200ms ease-out fade. Side panel slide: 300ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1).

component_architecture: <MetricsDashboard<TMetric>> generic. <MetricStrip metric={...} comparison={...} /> renders one strip. <Sparkline points={...} highlightEnd={true} /> is the inline sparkline. <DetailPanel metric={selected} onClose={...} /> for the slide-in. Each metric defines its 'direction' ('up-is-good' or 'down-is-good') so delta colors auto-resolve.
interaction_details: Each metric strip: label + 22px value + delta % with up/down arrow + 120px inline sparkline with endpoint dot. Three signals at a glance.
Delta color is direction-aware: revenue up = green; churn down = green. Computed from each metric's direction field.
Value update tweens via digit-roll animation (only changed digits animate up from below over 600ms). NEVER a flash or whole-strip re-render.
Click strip: side panel slides in from right (300ms), dashboard dims to 60%. Esc closes. Other strips remain readable for context.
Comparison dropdown (top-right): changes the period EVERY delta references. One source of truth.
accessibility_considerations: Each strip is a real <button> with descriptive aria-label including value AND percentage change in plain English ('Revenue: $4,200, 12.3 percent change'). Sparkline is decorative; the aria-label conveys the trend numerically. Delta direction (up-is-good vs down-is-good) is encoded in aria-label as 'improvement' or 'decline'. Side panel is role='dialog' with focus trap and Esc dismissal. Reduced-motion: digit roll + panel slide become instant. Color is supplemented by arrow direction and text label.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Direction-aware delta colors — revenue-up and churn-down are both 'green' because they're both improvements; the metric knows its own goodness direction. 2) Inline sparkline with endpoint dot RIGHT NEXT to the value — shape and magnitude in the same visual field. 3) Digit-roll animation that only animates changed digits — value updates communicate change without re-rendering the strip.

title: Inline rich-text editor with slash menu and keyboard-only block manipulation
design_brief: You're building the rich-text editor for a docs/notes app (think Notion's editor, Linear's issue description, Coda's blocks). Pain point: most rich-text editors require constant mouse trips to a toolbar; the slash menu fixed that for block creation but most editors still need mouse for moving blocks or transforming them. The bar: Notion's editor (the canonical slash menu + keyboard model), Coda's block manipulation, Linear's issue editor.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Floating toolbar that follows selection (jumpy, breaks reading)
Slash menu that's a modal instead of an inline dropdown
Drag-handles only revealed on hover (mouse-required)
Cmd+B for bold but no shortcut for headings
No way to move a block up/down via keyboard
Slash menu that fuzzy-searches but doesn't accept partial matches
ux_reasoning:
Notion's slash-menu was a UX leap. We extend it with full keyboard control of block movement and transformation — so power users never need the mouse.

Slash menu: typing / at the start of an empty line opens an inline command popover anchored to that line. Fuzzy-search by typing more (/head matches 'Heading 1', 'Heading 2', etc.). Arrow keys navigate, Enter selects, Esc closes (deleting the /). Categories shown with collapsed groups: Text, Heading, List, Media, Embed.

Block manipulation shortcuts: focused on a block, Cmd+Shift+ArrowUp/Down moves the block up/down. Cmd+Shift+Enter on a list item indents/outdents. Cmd+Shift+8 transforms current block to a bulleted list. Cmd+Shift+7 to numbered list. Cmd+Alt+1/2/3 to heading levels. All Notion-compatible.

Markdown shortcuts: typing '# ' at line start transforms to H1. '## ' → H2. '* ' or '- ' → bullet list. '1. ' → numbered. '> ' → quote. '``` ' → code block. Real-time transformation as the user types — they don't need to know the slash menu exists.

Focused-block menu: when a block has focus, a tiny 'handle' (6 dots) appears 24px to the left of the block. Clicking or pressing Tab on the handle opens a contextual menu (duplicate, delete, transform, color). Hover-only is FORBIDDEN; the handle is always present when the block is focused.

Inline formatting: select text, Cmd+B/I/U for bold/italic/underline. Cmd+K opens link popover. NO floating toolbar — the keyboard is the toolbar. For mouse users, hover-revealed mini-toolbar that respects the selection rect.

Motion: zero for block transformations (instant). Slash menu appears in 80ms opacity. Block move via Cmd+Shift+arrow: 120ms ease-out reorder with FLIP.

Rejected: a 'Tools' top toolbar that's always visible. Adds chrome for novices and never used by experts. Hover-revealed mini-toolbar + slash menu is the right scope. Also rejected: a 'beginner mode' that shows all shortcuts. Power users will read once and remember; novices use the slash menu.

design_tokens.colors: Block hover bg: oklch(0.97 0.005 250). Block handle: oklch(0.6 0.005 250) at 60% opacity, full on hover. Slash menu: white with 1px border, shadow. Selected suggestion: oklch(0.94 0.04 250).
design_tokens.typography: Body: Inter 16px / 26px (book-like). H1: 30px medium. H2: 24px medium. H3: 18px medium. Code: 'JetBrains Mono' 14px on oklch(0.96 0.005 250) bg.
design_tokens.spacing: Block gap: 4px. Handle position: 24px left of block. Slash menu: 280px wide, 320px max height.
design_tokens.motion: Slash menu open: 80ms ease-out opacity. Block move: 120ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1) FLIP. Inline format toggle: instant.

component_architecture: <Editor blocks={[]} onChange={...} /> root. <Block /> per block (paragraph, heading, list, code, etc.) — generic over block type via discriminated union. <SlashMenu anchor={...} query={...} onPick={...} />. useBlockReordering() handles Cmd+Shift+arrow with FLIP. useInlineFormatting() handles Cmd+B/I/U on contentEditable.
interaction_details: Type / at start of empty block: inline slash menu opens below, fuzzy-searches by additional typing. Arrow keys navigate, Enter picks.
Markdown prefix transformation: typing '# ' transforms block to H1; '* ' to bullet list; '``` ' to code block.
Cmd+Shift+Up/Down: moves the current block up or down (with FLIP animation in surrounding blocks).
Block handle (6 dots) appears 24px left of focused block — keyboard-accessible via Tab.
Cmd+B/I/U toggles inline formatting on selection; Cmd+K opens link popover. No floating toolbar competes.
accessibility_considerations: All blocks are real semantic HTML (<h1>, <ul>, <blockquote>, <pre>) — screen readers traverse the document structure natively. Slash menu uses role='listbox' / role='option'. Block handle is a real <button> with aria-label='Block options'. ContentEditable accessibility: aria-multiline='true' on the root. Keyboard nav: Tab moves between blocks; arrow keys move within. Reduced-motion: slash menu fade and block move FLIP become instant. Color is supplementary throughout.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Real-time markdown prefix transformation ('# ' becomes H1) — power users type markdown; the editor transforms in place. 2) Cmd+Shift+arrow moves blocks with FLIP-choreographed reorder — full block manipulation from keyboard, no mouse required. 3) Slash menu is inline (anchored to the block) not modal — never breaks reading flow.

title: Shared whiteboard with smooth pencil strokes and conflict-free shape co-editing
design_brief: You're building a collaborative whiteboard for remote workshops (think Miro, FigJam, tldraw). Multiple users drawing, dragging shapes, and writing sticky notes simultaneously. Pain point: most collab whiteboards either lag on stroke rendering (latency feels terrible) or have shape-conflict bugs where two users dragging the same sticky create chaos. The bar: tldraw (the gold standard for smooth web canvas), FigJam, Miro at its best.
