anti_patterns_to_avoid: Stroke rendering with whole-canvas redraw per pointer event (kills fps)
Server round-trip required before showing your own stroke
Shape selection that doesn't show who else has it selected
Locking shapes during another user's drag (frustrating)
Generic 'X is drawing' notification banner
Pan/zoom that don't sync to other users via 'follow user' feature
ux_reasoning:
Smooth strokes are non-negotiable. Three rendering layers:

1. LOCAL PREDICTION: as the user draws, the stroke renders on a TEMPORARY canvas layer at 60Hz using direct pointer events. No network delay. No React.

2. NETWORK: pointer positions broadcast at 30Hz (throttled). Other users see the stroke in-progress on their LIVE layer (different from your local).

3. COMMITTED: when stroke completes (pointerup), the final smoothed path serializes and writes to the shared doc (Y.js / Liveblocks). Other users see the committed stroke appear on the persistent layer; their LIVE layer for that user clears.

Stroke smoothing: incoming pointer events are too jittery to render directly. We apply Catmull-Rom spline interpolation between every 4th point (decimation + smoothing combined). For pressure-sensitive devices: pressure modulates stroke width.

Shape conflict-free editing: each shape has a single owner during drag (the user who pointerdown'd first wins; others see the shape locked with their cursor color outline). When the owner releases, others' attempts to grab the shape in the meantime are silently dropped (the user just clicks again). This is the optimistic-with-server-arbitration pattern.

Shape selection presence: when User A selects a shape, others see a colored selection outline in A's user color (4px, dashed). Multiple users selecting same shape: each color stacks as concentric outlines.

'Follow user' mode: clicking a user's avatar in the presence stack enters follow mode — your pan/zoom locks to theirs as they navigate. Useful for workshop facilitation. Esc exits.

Motion: zero on commits. Stroke fade-in on commit: instant. Selection outline transition (color shift): 80ms ease-out.

Rejected: a 'undo all my edits' bulk action. Too dangerous in collab — undo is per-action, per-user, via Cmd+Z (local stack). Also rejected: AI-generated shapes from rough strokes. Off-topic from the core collab problem; can be a future feature.

design_tokens.colors: Canvas bg: oklch(0.99 0.005 60) (warm paper). Grid dots: oklch(0.92 0.005 60). User colors: standard 12-palette. Sticky notes: pastel oklch variants (0.92 0.08 60/145/250/etc) — never saturated.
design_tokens.typography: Sticky note: Inter 14px / 20px. User name on avatar: 10px. Tool palette: 11px medium.
design_tokens.spacing: Tool palette: 48px wide. Avatar stack: top-right with 24px circles. Grid dot spacing: 24px.
design_tokens.motion: Stroke render: 60Hz raf during draw. Commit transition: instant. Selection outline: 80ms ease-out color shift. Pan/zoom follow: 150ms ease-out lerp to target.

component_architecture: <Whiteboard /> with three canvas layers (committed, live-other-users, local-prediction). useStrokeBroadcast() throttles pointer positions. useShapeLock() handles shape ownership during drag. useFollowMode() locks pan/zoom to another user. State for committed shapes lives in shared doc; live state in refs.
interaction_details: Draw stroke: local canvas renders at 60Hz directly via pointer events (no React). No latency.
Stroke broadcasts at 30Hz to other users; they see your in-progress stroke on their LIVE layer.
On pointerup: smoothed Catmull-Rom path commits to shared doc; appears on persistent layer for all users; LIVE layer clears.
Click shape: selection outline appears in your user color (4px dashed). Others see your selection too. Multiple selectors stack as concentric outlines.
Click another user's avatar: enters Follow mode; your pan/zoom locks to theirs with 150ms lerp. Esc exits.
accessibility_considerations: Canvas has aria-label describing the whiteboard. A parallel <ul> lists all committed shapes (text/sticky notes/strokes) for screen reader access. Selection state of shapes is announced via aria-live='polite'. Keyboard: arrow keys nudge selected shape, Delete removes, Cmd+Z undoes. Reduced-motion: pan/zoom follow becomes instant. Color: user identification is supplemented by avatar tooltip names on cursor + selection.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Three-layer canvas (committed / live-other-users / local-prediction) — your strokes appear at 60Hz with zero latency while others see your in-progress at 30Hz. 2) Catmull-Rom spline smoothing on raw pointer points — strokes feel pen-like, not pixel-jittery. 3) Shape ownership lock during drag (pointerdown-first wins) with optimistic UI for everyone else — no conflict bugs.

title: File upload with chunked progress, error recovery, and inline cancel-to-resume
design_brief: You're building file upload for a creative collaboration tool (think Loom uploads, Frame.io uploads, Notion file attach). Pain point: most uploaders show a single linear progress bar that lies (% jumps in chunks, doesn't account for network), and cancellation discards the upload. The bar: Loom's chunked upload, Frame.io's resumable uploads, Google Drive's reliable upload pipeline.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Single linear progress bar with no time estimate
Cancel button that discards all upload progress
Modal upload window blocking other work
Generic 'Upload failed' with no recovery path
Spinner alongside progress bar (redundant)
No feedback that file was sliced into chunks
ux_reasoning:
Uploads should feel resilient. Network blips happen; the UX should make them invisible.

Chunked transfer: files >5MB split into 5MB chunks. Each chunk uploads independently and gets a checksum. The progress bar shows real cumulative bytes (not 'phase 1 of 3' lies). Time estimate computed from rolling 5-chunk average speed.

Progress visualization: a horizontal bar in the file row, divided into chunks (visible if file >5MB). Each chunk is a small segment: pending = oklch(0.85) gray, uploading = oklch(0.55 0.18 250) blue with shimmer, complete = oklch(0.55 0.14 145) green, failed = oklch(0.6 0.18 25) red. The chunked visual is honest about parallel transfer.

Error recovery: chunk failures retry automatically up to 3 times with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s). After 3 fails, that chunk shows red and a 'Retry chunk' inline button. The other chunks continue. Partial uploads PERSIST — refresh the page, the upload resumes from the last successful chunk.

Cancel-to-pause: 'Cancel' is replaced by 'Pause'. Paused uploads keep their chunks; clicking 'Resume' continues. The user can pause to free bandwidth for something else, then resume. To truly discard: long-press the X (1s confirmation).

Queue behavior: 3 uploads run concurrently. Additional uploads queue. The user sees the queue visually as a stack — uploading items first, queued items below in 'waiting' state.

Motion: progress bar fill is real-time (no transitions). Chunk completion: 80ms color fade from blue to green. Retry indicator: 1.5s slow pulse.

Rejected: showing a big modal during upload. Lets the user keep working; uploads live in a small persistent panel at the bottom of the screen that can collapse/expand. Also rejected: showing transfer speed in MB/s. Useful for power users but most don't care; time-remaining is what matters.

design_tokens.colors: Chunk pending: oklch(0.88 0.005 250). Chunk uploading: oklch(0.55 0.18 250). Chunk done: oklch(0.55 0.14 145). Chunk failed: oklch(0.6 0.18 25). Panel bg: oklch(0.18 0.005 250 / 0.95) backdrop-blur.
design_tokens.typography: File name: Inter 13px medium. Status text: 12px regular. Time remaining: 11px tabular-nums.
design_tokens.spacing: Upload row: 48px tall. Chunk segments: 2px gap. Panel: 320px wide, anchored bottom-right.
design_tokens.motion: Progress fill: real-time (no transition). Chunk done color: 80ms ease-out. Retry pulse: 1500ms ease-in-out infinite. Panel collapse/expand: 200ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1).

component_architecture: <UploadManager /> root with <UploadPanel /> renders the persistent panel. useUploadQueue() manages concurrency (3 parallel). useChunkedUpload(file) handles slicing, parallel chunk uploads with retry, progress aggregation. Each <UploadRow /> shows file name + chunked progress + actions.
interaction_details: Drop file: appears in panel with chunked progress bar (2px segments visible if file >5MB).
Each chunk uploads in parallel (up to 3 concurrent). Failed chunks retry with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s).
After 3 retries: chunk turns red, inline 'Retry chunk' button appears. Other chunks continue.
Pause: stops new chunks; in-flight finish. Resume: continues from last successful chunk.
Page refresh: paused/in-progress uploads resume from chunk-level checkpoint.
accessibility_considerations: Each progress bar uses role='progressbar' with aria-valuenow/min/max. Failed chunks have aria-label='Retry chunk N' on the inline button. Status text ('40% · 12s remaining') is updated via aria-live='polite' but throttled to every 5s (no announcement spam). Retry/cancel actions are real buttons. Reduced-motion: shimmer and pulse animations disabled (chunk colors remain informative). Color: chunk status supplemented by aria-label per chunk.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Chunked progress bar with per-chunk visual state (pending/uploading/done/failed) — honest about parallel transfer, not a single lying bar. 2) Per-chunk retry with exponential backoff; failed chunks show inline retry button; other chunks continue independently — partial-progress resilience. 3) Pause (not Cancel) is the primary destructive action; true cancel requires long-press — prevents accidental discard of large uploads.

title: Scrollytelling article with synced chart annotation reveal
design_brief: You're building a data journalism long-read (think Pudding.cool's interactive essays, Bloomberg Graphics, NYT's Upshot pieces). Pain point: most 'scrollytelling' implementations are either heavy parallax that fights the user's scroll, or text + chart that don't actually coordinate. The bar: Pudding.cool's articles, Bloomberg's scrollytelling, FiveThirtyEight's election analysis.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Parallax that overrides browser scroll
Sticky chart that updates with a 500ms delay
Chart that doesn't communicate which paragraph it's responding to
Animations triggered on viewport enter that auto-play even if user scrolls past
Hijacked scroll that snaps between sections
Chart annotations that just fade in/out without explanation
ux_reasoning:
Scrollytelling is text-led: the user reads at their pace, and the chart RESPONDS by updating its state. The chart never leads; the chart never demands.

Layout: sticky chart on the LEFT (40% of viewport width), scrolling text on the RIGHT (60%). The chart is pinned for the duration of the section; once the user scrolls past, the chart releases and the next section's chart pins in. CSS position: sticky does this with no JS.

Text paragraphs are annotated with data-step attributes. As each paragraph enters a 'trigger zone' (vertical middle of viewport ±50px), an IntersectionObserver fires, and the chart transitions to the new state corresponding to that step. States might be: 'highlight bar 3', 'overlay annotation', 'transition to log scale', etc.

Transitions between chart states: 800ms tweens (longer than typical UI — chart changes are story moments). Easing: cubic-bezier(0.65, 0, 0.35, 1) — symmetric ease. The chart 'breathes' between states.

Annotation reveal: text labels appear ON the chart at relevant points, with a thin connecting line. Annotations fade in over 300ms when their step is active; fade out over 200ms when leaving.

No scroll hijack: the user can scroll past sections quickly. The chart updates as fast as the user scrolls. We don't force a 'pause' on each section. The reader is in control.

Reduced-motion: chart state changes become instant. All annotations remain visible at all times (not faded in/out per step) so the user can read everything statically.

Mobile: stacked layout (chart on top, text below). Chart becomes 60vh tall and pinned at top during section reading. Tap-to-advance through chart states for users who prefer it.

Rejected: full-screen scroll-driven transitions where each section takes over the whole viewport. Beautiful demo, but ruins readability for long-form. Pinned chart + scrolling text is the right balance. Also rejected: requiring video for the chart states. Static SVG transitions are smaller, sharper, and faster.

design_tokens.colors: Chart bg: oklch(0.99 0.005 60). Text bg: same (continuous). Active step text: oklch(0.18 0.005 60). Inactive text: oklch(0.18 0.005 60) (same — never dim past text). Annotation lines: oklch(0.55 0.005 250) 1px. Highlighted data: oklch(0.55 0.18 250).
design_tokens.typography: Body: serif (Tiempos / Newsreader) 18px / 30px. Annotations: Inter 12px on chart. Caption: 13px italic.
design_tokens.spacing: Chart pane: 40% width × 100vh. Text pane: 60% width with 24px gutters. Section: minimum 100vh tall for proper pinning.
design_tokens.motion: Chart state transitions: 800ms cubic-bezier(0.65, 0, 0.35, 1). Annotation fade-in: 300ms ease-out. Annotation fade-out: 200ms ease-in.

component_architecture: <Scrollytelling sections={...}> renders text + sticky chart. Each section has { id, paragraphs: [{ text, stepId }], chartStates: { stepId: renderer } }. useStepObserver() tracks which step is currently in the trigger zone via IntersectionObserver. <Chart currentStep={...} states={...} /> renders the appropriate state for the current step.
interaction_details: Scroll through text: paragraphs trigger chart state changes via IntersectionObserver when entering vertical-center zone.
Chart transitions over 800ms with symmetric ease (cubic-bezier(0.65, 0, 0.35, 1)) — slow enough to feel like a story moment.
Annotations fade in (300ms) on relevant step, fade out (200ms) when leaving.
Mobile: chart stacks above text, becomes 60vh sticky at top of each section.
Reduced-motion: chart updates instantly; all annotations remain visible at all times (no fade in/out).
accessibility_considerations:
Each paragraph is a real

— screen readers read the article normally. Chart has aria-label describing current state ('Bar chart showing population by state. Currently highlighting California: 39.5 million'). Annotations are in SVG — readable to screen readers when announced. Reduced-motion: all chart transitions disabled, annotations always visible (so SR users get the full data without state-by-state announcements). Color: highlighted bar is supplemented by annotation text + aria-label.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) IntersectionObserver-driven step tracking — chart responds to text without hijacking scroll. 2) Sticky chart releases naturally on section change (CSS position: sticky) — no manual pinning logic. 3) Reduced-motion alternate: all annotations stay visible always, so screen reader users + reduced-motion users get the full story statically.

title: Tour-as-cursor: ambient hand pointer that demonstrates features without blocking
design_brief: You're building first-run feature discovery for a creative tool (think Figma's first-time prompts, Procreate's gesture introductions, Photoshop's first-launch tips). Pain point: spotlight overlays that dim the entire UI to highlight one button are jarring and patronizing. The bar: Apple's Final Cut intro (mostly invisible, contextually surfaces tips), Procreate's brilliant gesture cards, Figma's progressive feature discovery.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Spotlight overlay that dims the entire UI except one button
Multi-step modal tour with Next/Previous
Annotated popovers attached to every UI element
Coachmarks with arrows that obscure content
'Got it' button that has to be clicked to dismiss
Tour that auto-advances regardless of user action
ux_reasoning:
The user came to USE the app, not learn about it. Onboarding should AMBIENTLY demonstrate, then disappear. Procreate's first-launch is the gold standard — a quiet hand-pointer that shows you a gesture, then vanishes the moment you try it yourself.

Mechanics: on first launch, a translucent 'hand cursor' (custom SVG) appears in the relevant area of the canvas. It performs the gesture (e.g., two-finger tap to undo, or drag-from-toolbar) at 50% animation speed so the user can see what's happening. The hand fades out the moment the user makes ANY input. If the user repeats the demonstrated gesture, that lesson is marked complete and won't replay.

No blocking, no dimming, no modal: the hand-cursor is the entire UI of the tutorial. It's an overlay element with pointer-events: none, so the user can interact with the actual UI underneath at any time.

Lesson sequencing: lessons are queued in order of importance (most fundamental first). Each lesson has a 'trigger' — when the user enters a state where the lesson is relevant. So 'how to undo' doesn't appear until the user has made at least one action. 'How to pinch zoom' appears once they've drawn something larger than viewport. Context-aware, not chronological.

Progress: tucked away in a small 'Tips' button in the corner that, when clicked, shows a list of all tips with completed ones checked. The user can replay any tip on demand. Most users never click this; the tips show themselves at the right moments.

For desktop apps: the 'hand cursor' becomes an animated CURSOR + KEYBOARD KEY indicator showing the shortcut. Different visual, same principle: demonstrate, then vanish.

Motion: hand-cursor enters with 200ms ease-out opacity fade. Performs the gesture animation at half-speed (e.g., a two-finger pinch shown over 2s instead of 1s). Exits with 100ms fade.

Rejected: AI assistant that you can ask 'how do I do X?'. Useful, but a different mental model — ambient discovery is for users who don't even know what to ask.

design_tokens.colors: Hand cursor: oklch(0.18 0.005 250 / 0.6) with thin oklch(0.99 0.005 250 / 0.7) outline. Keyboard key indicator: oklch(0.18 0.005 250 / 0.85) bg with white text. Tips badge (corner): oklch(0.95 0.005 250) with subtle border.
design_tokens.typography: Keyboard key: 'JetBrains Mono' 12px in monospaced kbd-shape. Tips list item: Inter 13px regular.
design_tokens.spacing: Hand cursor: 40px wide. Keyboard key indicator: 28px tall pill shape. Tips badge: 32px circle in bottom-right.
design_tokens.motion: Hand cursor entrance: 200ms ease-out opacity. Gesture animation: 2x slower than natural speed for clarity. Cursor exit on user input: 100ms fade-out.

component_architecture: <AmbientTour /> root mounts globally with z-index above canvas but pointer-events: none. Each tour lesson is { id, trigger: (state) => bool, gesture: ReactNode (animated), targetElement: () => HTMLElement | null }. useTourQueue() manages which lesson is currently visible. useUserInputDetection() listens to any pointer/keyboard event and dismisses active lesson.
interaction_details: First time entering a context that's relevant for a tip (e.g., drawing for the first time → 'how to undo'): hand cursor fades in 200ms over the relevant area.
Hand cursor performs the gesture animation at 2x slower than natural speed.
User makes ANY input (pointer, keyboard): hand cursor fades out over 100ms, lesson marked complete in localStorage.
Tips badge (bottom-right): subtle '?' button; click to see all tips with check marks for completed.
Reduced-motion: hand cursor stays static (no animation); user dismisses by interacting.
accessibility_considerations: Hand cursor is aria-hidden — it's a visual demonstration that doesn't carry information for screen readers (the equivalent for SR users is verbal description in the Tips badge list). Tips badge is a real button with aria-label. Lesson descriptions are full sentences in the Tips list. Reduced-motion: cursor pulse animation disabled. The whole tour is non-blocking — keyboard navigation through the app continues normally during a lesson.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Hand cursor is the entire onboarding UI — no modal, no spotlight, no Next button. Demonstrate, then vanish. 2) Lessons are context-triggered, not chronological — 'how to undo' appears after the first user action, not on day 1. 3) Pointer-events: none on the overlay means the user can ignore the demo and just use the app — the lesson dismisses the moment they try.

title: Inline editable spreadsheet-style table with batch validation and atomic save
design_brief: You're building inline editing for a data-grid (think Airtable, Notion database, Linear's grid view). Users edit dozens of cells across multiple rows; each edit fires server validation. Pain point: most inline editors either save per-cell-blur (chatty network, mid-edit conflicts) or require a 'Save changes' button (lose context). The bar: Airtable (the canonical inline grid), Notion's database, Linear's grid.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Per-cell save on blur (10 cells = 10 network requests)
Modal 'Edit row' that loses the multi-cell context
Validation errors that appear only on save attempt
Generic 'Field required' without specifying which cell
Disabled tab navigation between cells while saving
'Save changes' button at top that requires scroll to reach
ux_reasoning:
Spreadsheet editing is bulk-by-nature. The UX should optimize for: rapid keyboard nav between cells, visible 'dirty' state per cell, batch validation before save, and atomic commit (all or none).

Dirty state visualization: an edited cell gets a 2px colored left border (oklch(0.55 0.18 250) = blue). The dirty state persists until save or revert. Multiple cells across multiple rows can be dirty simultaneously. A floating action bar appears at the bottom when ≥1 cell is dirty: '3 unsaved changes · Save · Discard'.

Validation: each cell has a type (text, number, date, select). Type validation happens locally on blur (e.g., '12abc' in a number column → red border + inline error '12abc isn't a number'). Cross-cell validation (e.g., 'end date must be after start date') runs on Save click and returns row-level errors.

Batch save: clicking Save sends ALL dirty cells in a single batch request. Server validates the whole batch; if any cell fails, the WHOLE batch is rolled back (atomic), and the action bar shows 'N changes failed validation — see highlighted cells' with red borders on the failures.

Keyboard navigation: Tab moves to next cell (right), Shift+Tab to previous. Enter commits cell + moves down. Esc reverts current cell (only) and keeps focus.

Undo: Cmd+Z undoes the last cell edit (per-cell undo, not bulk). Multiple Cmd+Z presses traverse the cell-edit stack. Works even after save (with server-side undo support).

Conflict resolution: if someone else edited a cell while you had it dirty, on save the server returns 409. We show inline 'Cell was changed by Alice' with two buttons: 'Keep mine' or 'Use theirs'. No modal, no full-page conflict resolver.

Motion: dirty border appears instantly (no transition). Save success: dirty borders fade to transparent over 300ms (the 'commit' moment). Save error: red border pulses once.

Rejected: live save with debounce (300ms after last keystroke). Tested it — feels good for single cells but creates race conditions for tab-fast users who edit 5 cells in 5 seconds. Explicit Save is more honest.

design_tokens.colors: Dirty cell border-left: 2px oklch(0.55 0.18 250). Validation error border-left: 2px oklch(0.6 0.18 25). Action bar: oklch(0.18 0.005 250) bg with white text. Conflict cell: 2px oklch(0.75 0.16 80) border.
design_tokens.typography: Cell content: Inter 13px / 20px. Action bar: 13px regular. Error text below cell: 11px regular.
design_tokens.spacing: Cell: 32px tall × variable width. Action bar: 48px tall, full width, anchored bottom. Dirty border: 2px wide on left edge of cell.
design_tokens.motion: Dirty border: instant. Save commit fade: 300ms ease-out. Validation error pulse: 200ms once. Action bar slide-up: 200ms ease-out.

component_architecture: <DataGrid<TRow extends BaseRow>> generic over row schema. <Cell /> renders one cell with focused/dirty/error state. <ActionBar /> renders when dirty cells exist. State: { dirtyCells: Map<rowId, Map<colId, newValue>>, errors: Map<cellKey, string> }. useBatchSave() handles the atomic submit.
interaction_details: Edit any cell: 2px blue left border appears instantly. Action bar at bottom shows 'N unsaved changes'.
Type validation on blur: number column with 'abc' input → red border + inline error below cell. Other cells unaffected.
Click Save: sends entire batch in one request. Atomic: all-or-nothing. Success: borders fade to transparent over 300ms. Failure: red borders on failed cells, action bar shows summary.
Cmd+Z: undoes the last cell edit (per-cell, not batch). Multiple presses traverse the stack.
Cross-user conflict: cell turns yellow border with inline 'Changed by X — Keep mine / Use theirs' buttons.
accessibility_considerations: Each cell is a real <input>. Validation errors use role='alert'. Action bar uses role='status' aria-live='polite'. Dirty state announced via aria-label on each cell ('Name, unsaved'). Save button disabled state has aria-disabled. Keyboard: Tab/Shift+Tab navigate cells; Enter commits and moves down; Esc reverts current cell. Reduced-motion: action bar slide and border fade become instant. Color: dirty/error state supplemented by aria-label and inline error text.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Atomic batch save — all dirty cells commit together or none commit, with cell-level error highlighting on failure. No 'half-saved' state. 2) Floating action bar appears only when dirty changes exist — never persistent chrome. 3) Conflict resolution is inline at the cell ('Keep mine / Use theirs'), not a modal — preserves multi-cell editing context.

title: 3D portfolio scene with depth-of-field focus and click-to-orbit project cards
design_brief: You're building the homepage for a designer's personal site or a creative agency (think Bruno Simon's portfolio, Brittany Chiang's elegant 2D portfolios, Studio Mesh's WebGL work). Pain point: most 3D portfolios are either gimmick-heavy (drive a 3D car around the site) or are 2D portfolios with a token 3D hero. The bar: Bruno Simon's portfolio (the gold standard for 3D as navigation), Active Theory's case studies, Studio Mesh's product showcases.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: 3D scene as background decoration (parallax stars)
'Press any key to start' before showing content
WASD controls for a portfolio (it's a website, not a game)
Loading bar that says '0% — initializing scene'
Auto-rotating camera that the user must wait to finish
No keyboard/SR alternative — pure WebGL experience
ux_reasoning:
3D for a portfolio earns its weight ONLY if navigation is more inviting than a grid. The user should feel they're entering a CURATED SPACE, not a 3D game.

Scene: a floating gallery of project cards arranged in 3D space at varying depths. Cards are textured planes (each shows project preview image + title). Cards drift slowly (subtle 0.1Hz sine wave on Y axis, different phase per card) — alive, not static.

Depth-of-field: a real DOF post-processing effect blurs cards that aren't near the camera's focal plane. The focal plane is at the depth of the card directly ahead. As you orbit, focus changes. This makes the scene feel cinematic and inverts the typical 'everything sharp' web aesthetic.

Navigation: drag to orbit the camera around the scene's center (where the cards are clustered). Mouse wheel zooms in/out. Click a card → camera smoothly orbits to face that card squarely at a closer distance over 800ms; card's project loads in a side overlay (60% transparent dark overlay on right 40% of screen with project details).

Navigation Constraints: camera is on a sphere of fixed radius around center (can orbit horizontally and vertically), but with a smaller radius when 'focused on a card'. Orbit angle has soft bounds (can't flip upside down) — this is the constrained-camera technique from Apple's product pages.

Project card hover: the card scales up by 5% over 300ms ease-out + the DOF focal plane snaps to that card's depth. So hovering pulls cards into focus.

Keyboard / SR alternative: Tab cycles between projects (with arrow keys to move between adjacent ones in 3D space — adjacency computed by angular distance). Enter opens the project. A skip-to-content link reveals a parallel <ul> of projects for full SR + keyboard browsing.

Motion: camera orbit on drag is 1:1 (no lerp). Orbit-to-card focus is 800ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). Cards drift on a slow sine wave. DOF focal plane lerps over 400ms.

Rejected: a hero animation that plays before the user can interact. Adds 4 seconds of nothing. Also rejected: physics-based card collisions if cards drift too close. Beautiful in demos, distracting in use.

design_tokens.colors: Scene bg: vertical gradient oklch(0.10 0.01 250) → oklch(0.05 0.01 280). Card frame: 1px oklch(0.99 0.005 250 / 0.2). Detail overlay: oklch(0.10 0.01 250 / 0.85) backdrop-blur.
design_tokens.typography: Card title rendered as canvas texture: Inter 18px medium white. Project detail body: serif 17px / 28px.
design_tokens.spacing: Cards arranged in a sphere of radius 4 units; ~15 cards. Detail overlay: right 40% of viewport.
design_tokens.motion: Drift: 10000ms sine. Orbit-to-card: 800ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). Hover scale: 300ms ease-out. DOF lerp: 400ms.

component_architecture: <Portfolio scene /> with <Canvas frameloop='demand'> + <EffectComposer> for DOF. <ProjectCard project={...} /> renders a textured plane. <OrbitCamera focusCardId={...} /> handles smooth camera tweens. <DetailOverlay project={selected} /> renders HTML over canvas.
interaction_details: Drag canvas: camera orbits around the cluster of cards. Soft phi bounds prevent flipping upside down.
Hover a card: scales up 5% over 300ms; DOF focal plane snaps to that card's depth so it becomes sharp while others blur further.
Click a card: camera smoothly orbits to face it squarely at closer radius (800ms ease-out). Detail overlay slides in from right.
Detail overlay: dark glass with project text + thumbnail. Close button (or Esc) returns camera to default orbit.
Cards drift slowly on Y axis at different phases — scene feels alive without demanding attention.
accessibility_considerations: Canvas has aria-label. A parallel <ul class='sr-only'> lists all projects as buttons — screen reader users get full keyboard access. Tab + Enter opens any project (same detail overlay as click). Detail overlay is role='dialog'. Reduced-motion: camera orbits become instant; card drift becomes static; DOF effect remains (visual style, not animation). Color is supplementary to the visible card thumbnails.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Depth-of-field post-processing with focal plane bound to hovered/focused card — cinematic 'pull focus' that makes navigation feel intentional. 2) Camera lives on a sphere with soft phi bounds — orbit feels free but never disorients. 3) Parallel sr-only <ul> of all projects with full keyboard access — 3D scene + accessibility coexist.

title: Map-based search with bottom-sheet results on mobile and side panel on desktop
design_brief: You're building a places-discovery app (think Airbnb, Foursquare, Apple Maps). Pain point: most map+results UIs either dedicate too much to map (results cramped) or too much to list (map becomes ornamental). The bar: Airbnb's map view (the gold standard), Foursquare City Guide, Apple Maps' search results.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Toggle button to switch between map and list view (forces choice)
Map markers all the same color and size (impossible to find your target)
Results list that requires scroll, map that doesn't pan with results
Modal place details that obscure both map AND list
Touch-and-hold to drop pin — undiscoverable
Auto-pan to user's location every time the app opens
ux_reasoning:
A map + list is fundamentally bimodal: SCAN (look at map, see geographic distribution) and EXPLORE (read list, compare details). The UI must serve both without forcing a choice.

Desktop layout: map on right (60%), results list on left (40%). Hovering a list item highlights the corresponding map marker; clicking centers map on it without zooming (preserve overview). Map markers are clusters at low zoom; individual pins at high zoom. Each pin shows price or rating directly (Airbnb-style).

Mobile layout: map fills the screen; results in a bottom sheet that has three snap points: peek (15% height, shows just the count), default (50%), expanded (90%). User can drag the sheet to any snap point. The sheet's content scrolls when at expanded snap. This is the Apple Maps + Google Maps mobile pattern, perfected.

Mobile bottom sheet behavior: when at peek, dragging UP expands to default; UP again expands to full. Drag DOWN from any snap dismisses to peek (never fully off-screen; you can always get back to results). The handle (small bar at top of sheet) is the drag affordance — also tappable to cycle through snaps.

Search bar: top of map on both. Type to search; results live-update as you type (debounced 200ms). Recent searches in a dropdown below the input.

Filters: small pill bar below search input. Each pill: 'Price', 'Rating', 'Open now'. Tapping a pill opens a popover for that filter category. Active filters show pill in inverted color with X to remove.

Motion: bottom sheet drag is 1:1; snap-to-nearest on release with 300ms cubic-bezier(0.32, 0.72, 0, 1) (the iOS sheet curve). Map pan when clicking list item: 400ms ease-out.

Rejected: a full-screen list view with no map. Real users in research said 'when I want a list I open Yelp' — the map IS the reason to use a places app. Also rejected: AR mode by default. Cool for tourism, friction for everyday.

design_tokens.colors: Map: standard tile colors (Mapbox default or similar). Pin default: oklch(0.55 0.18 250) (brand). Pin highlighted: oklch(0.45 0.18 25) (warm red). Pin cluster: white bg with brand color border. Bottom sheet bg: oklch(0.99 0.005 250).
design_tokens.typography: Search input: 16px regular (mobile = avoids zoom-on-focus). Place name in list: 15px medium. Meta (rating, distance): 12px regular oklch(0.55).
design_tokens.spacing: Mobile sheet snap points: 15vh / 50vh / 90vh. Sheet handle: 36×4px centered with 12px padding. Desktop side panel: 400px fixed.
design_tokens.motion: Sheet drag: 1:1. Snap on release: 300ms cubic-bezier(0.32, 0.72, 0, 1). Map pan: 400ms ease-out. Pin highlight: 80ms color change.

component_architecture: <MapSearch /> root with shared SearchContext. <MobileMapSearch /> uses <BottomSheet snapPoints={[0.15, 0.5, 0.9]} />. <DesktopMapSearch /> uses fixed side panel. Both render the same <PlacesList /> + <Map />. useMapMarkerHighlight() binds list hover to map highlight.
interaction_details: Desktop: hover list item → corresponding map pin highlights in warm red. Click → map pans to center (no zoom change, preserves overview).
Mobile: bottom sheet with 3 snap points (15% / 50% / 90%). Drag handle to cycle, or drag freely with snap-on-release.
Mobile: tap handle to cycle through snaps. Drag down from any snap returns to peek (15%) — never dismisses fully.
Search bar at top on both. Live-update results as you type (200ms debounce).
Filter pills below search; tap opens popover for that filter. Active filters show inverted color + X.
accessibility_considerations: Map has aria-label='Map showing search results' and pins are real <button>s with aria-label. Bottom sheet snap state is announced via aria-live. List uses role='list' / role='listitem' with proper semantics. Search input has aria-label. Keyboard nav on desktop: Tab through list → Enter opens place. Reduced-motion: sheet snap transitions become instant. Color: pin highlight color supplemented by aria-label + position change.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Mobile bottom sheet with 3 snap points + tap-handle-to-cycle — three densities of result browsing without leaving the map context. 2) Hover list item → map pin highlights AND vice versa — bidirectional binding makes the two views feel like one. 3) Click list item pans map without zooming — preserves the overview that makes a map useful.

title: Modal dialog with focus trap, restore, and inert background
design_brief: You're building the modal primitive used across an entire B2B app (think Linear's modals, GitHub's dialogs, Apple's HIG sheets). Pain point: most modal libraries either fail focus management on close OR don't properly inert the background, letting screen readers wander into the page. The bar: Radix UI's Dialog primitive, WAI-ARIA APG dialog pattern, GitHub's dialogs.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Modal close that returns focus to body
Background scrollable while modal is open
Tab cycling that escapes the modal
Esc that doesn't close the modal
Modal that traps focus with no visible close button
Multiple stacked modals fighting for focus
ux_reasoning:
A modal is more than a centered div. It's a focus boundary, a scroll boundary, an interaction boundary. Done right, screen reader users feel safe; sighted users feel oriented.

Focus management:
1. On open: previous focus owner pushed to a stack. Focus moves to the modal's first focusable element (or a data-autofocus-marked element, or the close button as fallback).
2. While open: Tab and Shift+Tab cycle WITHIN the modal — never escape.
3. On close (any reason: Esc, click outside, close button, programmatic): focus pops from stack, returns to the opener.
4. If the opener is no longer in the DOM (e.g., it was a button in a deleted row): focus falls back to a safe location (the main heading, then body).

Background inertness: when modal opens, all sibling elements get inert='' attribute. This excludes them from focus, screen reader announcement, and pointer events. The MODAL itself is the only interactive thing. Critical: use the `inert` attribute, NOT aria-hidden — inert correctly handles BOTH focus AND screen reader exclusion in one declaration.

Scroll lock: body gets overflow: hidden + a scrollbar-width-compensating padding-right (so the page doesn't shift when scrollbar disappears).

Nested modals: focus stack handles them. Each opens, pushes; closes, pops. We allow nesting but it's a code smell — modal-from-modal is usually a sign of bad UX. We warn in dev if nesting depth > 2.

Close paths: Esc (always), close button (always), click backdrop (optional via prop, off by default for destructive-action modals), programmatic onClose.

Visual design: subtle backdrop (60% opacity dark), modal centered with 24px padding, max-width responsive. Animation: 150ms ease-out opacity + 8px translateY on open; reverse on close. Reduced-motion: no animation.

Rejected: 'soft modals' that don't trap focus (sometimes called popovers). Different primitive, different name. Modals trap; popovers don't. Mixing them confuses users.

design_tokens.colors: Backdrop: oklch(0.18 0.005 250 / 0.6). Modal bg: oklch(0.99 0.005 250) light / oklch(0.18 0.005 250) dark. Title: oklch(0.18 0.005 250). Border: 1px oklch(0.92 0.005 250). Close icon: oklch(0.55 0.005 250).
design_tokens.typography: Title: Inter 17px medium. Body: 14px regular. Close button: visually hidden text + 16px icon.
design_tokens.spacing: Padding: 24px. Min-width: 320px. Max-width: 540px responsive. Close button: 32px square in top-right.
design_tokens.motion: Open: 150ms cubic-bezier(0, 0, 0.2, 1) opacity + 8px translateY. Close: 100ms cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 1, 1).

component_architecture: <Modal open onClose title> as the primitive. Uses useFocusTrap, useFocusStack, useScrollLock, useEscKey hooks. Renders into a portal (document.body) so it escapes any CSS clipping. Sibling elements (all other body children) get inert='' attribute while open.
interaction_details: Open: previous focus owner pushed; focus moves to first focusable (or data-autofocus marked element); background elements get inert; body scroll locked.
Tab / Shift+Tab: cycle within the modal — never escape.
Esc: closes modal. Click outside (if closeOnBackdrop): closes. Close button: closes.
On close: focus pops from stack to the opener. If opener is gone, focus falls back to <h1>.
Multiple modals: each push/pop independently via the focus stack. Dev warns in dev mode if nesting >2.
accessibility_considerations: role='dialog' aria-modal='true' aria-labelledby={titleId} — the canonical pattern. Background uses inert='' (modern, correct way to exclude everything in one shot). Focus trap implemented at keyboard level. Scroll lock prevents body scroll behind modal. Close button has aria-label. Reduced-motion: open animation disabled (modal appears instantly). Focus ring on interactive elements is 2px outline. Compatible with VoiceOver, NVDA, JAWS, TalkBack.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Uses the modern `inert` attribute on background siblings — correctly excludes focus AND screen reader announcement in one declaration (no aria-hidden tricks needed). 2) Focus restoration falls back to <h1> if the opener is no longer in DOM — never strands the user on body. 3) Scrollbar-width compensation on body padding-right prevents the page from shifting when scrollbar disappears.

title: Infinite scroll with virtual rendering, scroll restoration, and skeleton match
design_brief: You're building the feed view for a social product (think Twitter/X timeline, Instagram, Linear notifications). 10k items must scroll smoothly; users navigate away to a detail view and BACK to the exact scroll position. Pain point: most infinite scrolls either lag at 1k items (no virtualization) or have broken scroll restoration (back-button lands you at top). The bar: Twitter's timeline (best-in-class), Instagram's grid, Linear's notifications.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Rendering all items in the DOM (10k = janky scroll)
Skeleton placeholder that's a different height than real items (layout shift on load)
Spinner at the bottom waiting for more items (visible pause)
Scroll restoration that uses scrollIntoView (jumps, doesn't preserve exact offset)
Loading next page only AFTER reaching the bottom (sees the bottom)
Modal item details that lose scroll context
ux_reasoning:
Smooth infinite scroll has three pillars: virtualization, ahead-of-time fetch, and scroll restoration.

Virtualization: only render items in the viewport ± 2 viewport heights. Items above/below the rendered window are virtual (DOM elements removed). Container has a calculated total height so scrollbar is accurate. We use a variable-height implementation with per-item height measurement via ResizeObserver (cached in a Map keyed by item ID).

Ahead-of-time fetch: when the user is within 1.5 viewports of the bottom, fetch the next page. By the time they reach the bottom, items are loaded. They never see a 'loading more' state. Triggers via IntersectionObserver on a sentinel element placed 1.5 viewports below the last item.

Scroll restoration: when user navigates to detail view + back, restore scroll to the EXACT pixel offset they left. Naive scrollIntoView jumps. We save scrollTop on navigation away (in sessionStorage keyed by route + scroll-position-id) and restore on mount. If the item they had at top is no longer in the loaded set (e.g., page changed), we re-fetch the page containing it and restore.

Skeleton match: when a new page loads, skeletons appear that match the real item's layout exactly (text widths matching average lengths, image aspect ratios matching average). On data hydration, skeleton swap is instant — no layout shift, no fade. The eye doesn't notice the transition.

Motion: zero on scroll. Item insertion: instant. Skeleton-to-real swap: instant. The only visible motion is the natural scroll itself.

Optimistic actions: liking, saving, etc. update locally instantly; server confirmation in background. If failure: item state reverts with a small inline toast on that item ('Couldn't save — Retry').

Rejected: 'load more' button at bottom. Modern users expect infinite scroll for feeds; manual pagination feels like 2008. Detail views, sure — for feeds, infinite. Also rejected: pull-to-refresh on desktop. It's a mobile pattern; desktop has a refresh icon in the header.

design_tokens.colors: Item bg: oklch(0.99 0.005 250). Skeleton: oklch(0.93 0.005 250). Hover: oklch(0.97 0.005 250). Border between items: 0.5px oklch(0.93 0.005 250). NO shimmer on skeleton — too distracting in a feed.
design_tokens.typography: Item content matches design. Skeleton uses the same line-heights so heights match.
design_tokens.spacing: Item: variable height. Sentinel for fetch trigger: 1px wrapper 1.5 viewports below last item.
design_tokens.motion: None on scroll or item insertion. Skeleton-to-real: instant swap.

component_architecture: <VirtualFeed<TItem extends BaseItem>> generic. <FeedItem item={...} onMeasure={...} /> reports its height. <Sentinel onIntersect={...} /> for fetch trigger. useVirtualScroll() hook returns visible-item indices given scroll position + cached heights. useScrollRestoration() saves/restores scroll on route changes.
interaction_details: Scroll through 10k items: only ~30 are rendered at any time (visible + overscan). Heights are cached per-id; scrollbar position is accurate.
User reaches 1.5 viewports from bottom: next page fetches in background. By the time they reach the bottom, items are there.
Navigate to detail view + back: scroll restores to exact pixel offset via sessionStorage. If the top item is no longer loaded, fetches its page first.
Skeleton items have heights matching real items (line-heights tuned to averages). Hydration is an instant swap — no layout shift, no fade.
Like/save: optimistic update; failure shows inline toast on the item with Retry button.
accessibility_considerations: role='feed' on the scroll container (WAI-ARIA pattern for infinite-scroll feeds). Each item has aria-posinset + aria-setsize so screen readers know position in the larger list. Loading next page is announced via aria-busy + aria-live='polite'. Keyboard: arrow keys navigate items; PageUp/Down scroll viewport; Home/End jump. Reduced-motion: no motion to disable (no item-insertion animations).
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Variable-height virtualization with per-item height cache via ResizeObserver — scrollbar always accurate, items wrap correctly. 2) Scroll restoration via sessionStorage + auto-fetch missing page — back-button lands at exact pixel offset. 3) Skeleton heights match real item heights — zero layout shift on hydration, perceived as instant.

title: Minimalist Japanese MA-aesthetic settings panel with ample negative space
design_brief: You're building the settings UI for a meditation app or a high-end consumer product (think Calm, Headspace, MUJI's product pages, Apple Music's settings). The aesthetic brief: 'ma' (間) — the Japanese concept of meaningful negative space. Pain point: most settings panels are dense forms with adjacent rows. The bar: MUJI's web presence, Apple's late-2010s product pages, Karpf.studio for typography, Naoto Fukasawa's product design ethos.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Section dividers everywhere (chops the page into boxes)
Cards with shadow/border for each setting (visual noise)
Toggles in pastel colors
Icons next to every setting label
Default 16px gaps (cramped for this aesthetic)
Standard 14px text (too small for ma-aesthetic)
ux_reasoning:
Ma is not 'lots of whitespace'. It's INTENTIONAL space that gives meaning to the elements it separates. The relationships between elements become as important as the elements themselves.

Layout: single column, max-width 480px, centered. Settings grouped by category but with NO visible dividers — just larger vertical gaps (96px) between groups and smaller gaps (48px) between settings within a group. The eye learns the grouping from rhythm alone.

Typography: serif (Lyon, Tiempos, Newsreader, or Source Serif) for setting labels at 18px. NO uppercase, NO tracking-wider. Description below in sans-serif (Inter Light) at 14px in muted color. The serif/sans pairing creates 'voice contrast' — the label is the WHAT, the description is the WHY.

Toggles: a minimal horizontal line with a small filled circle that slides. NO color change on toggle. NO background fill. Position alone communicates state: circle on left = off, circle on right = on. Inspired by physical switches in MUJI products.

Values: numeric values inline in setting rows (right-aligned) in serif italic for elegance — 'Brightness   ⟶   78%' with the value as a typographically distinct part of the line.

Motion: zero spring. Toggle slide is 200ms cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1). Value updates are immediate. The interface is calm; nothing competes for attention.

Color: severely restricted palette. White, near-black for type, ONE accent color used ONLY for the toggle filled circle (oklch(0.45 0.04 30) — a warm dark brown, evoking ink). Backgrounds are always pure white. Hover states: text shifts to slightly darker, no bg change.

Dark mode: NOT a brand-dark, not a pure black. A warm dark oklch(0.18 0.005 60), with off-white type oklch(0.92 0.005 60). The 'ink' accent shifts to oklch(0.85 0.04 30) (lighter warm).

Rejected: a search input at the top to filter settings. Adds functional chrome that fights the aesthetic. With only ~20 settings, search is unnecessary. Also rejected: a back button. Settings are accessed via a single nav action and dismissed with Esc or the system back.

design_tokens.colors: Light: bg oklch(0.99 0.003 80), text oklch(0.20 0.005 30), muted oklch(0.55 0.005 30), accent oklch(0.45 0.04 30). Dark: bg oklch(0.18 0.005 60), text oklch(0.92 0.005 60), muted oklch(0.62 0.005 60), accent oklch(0.85 0.04 30).
design_tokens.typography: Setting label: serif (e.g., 'Lyon Display' or 'Tiempos Text') 18px / 28px / regular. Description: 'Inter' 14px / 22px / light weight. Value: serif 18px italic.
design_tokens.spacing: Page padding: 64px top, 96px bottom. Group gap: 96px. Setting gap: 48px within group. Page max-width: 480px centered.
design_tokens.motion: Toggle slide: 200ms cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1). Value text: instant updates. NO bg transitions.

component_architecture: <SettingsPage groups={[]}> renders the column. <SettingGroup title={...} settings={[]} />. <SettingRow label description value control />. <MinimalToggle value onChange /> is the primitive. Settings are pure data — no icons, no badges.
interaction_details: Toggle click: filled circle slides from one end of the line to the other over 200ms. NO color change, NO bg fill. Position alone is state.
Group titles are smaller and lighter than setting labels — hierarchy by weight + size, not by box.
No dividers, no card borders. Groups separated by 96px gaps; settings within a group by 48px. The eye finds the structure.
Values render inline in serif italic to the left of toggles — typographic distinction without color.
Dark mode: warm dark bg + off-white text + warm dark-ink accent — NEVER pure black/white.
accessibility_considerations: role='switch' aria-checked on toggles. aria-label on each toggle includes the setting name. Group titles use <h2> with aria-labelledby on sections. Semantic HTML throughout (no role='banner', just real headings). Color contrast: body text at 12:1 (AAA). Reduced-motion: toggle slide becomes instant. Focus ring is a 2px outline at 4px offset (the larger offset respects the aesthetic). Type minimum: 14px (description). No interactive element below 14px.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Toggle is a horizontal line with a sliding filled circle — NO color change, NO background fill. Position alone communicates state. 2) 96px gaps between groups + 48px within — no visible dividers needed, the eye finds the structure from rhythm alone. 3) Serif/sans typographic pairing creates 'voice contrast': label is WHAT, description is WHY.

title: GitHub-style PR diff view with split mode and side-by-side review comments
design_brief: You're building the pull-request diff view for a code-review tool (think GitHub PRs, Graphite, Reviewable). Pain point: most diff viewers either show inline (compact but hard to compare) or side-by-side (clear but doubles vertical scrolling). The bar: GitHub's diff view (the canonical one), Graphite's stacked PRs, Reviewable's threaded review.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Diff that always shows ALL lines of a file (huge diffs unscrollable)
Comments in a sidebar disconnected from the diff lines
Generic '+' button to add comment that requires modal
No way to toggle between split and inline
Syntax highlighting that ignores the language
Folded sections that require multiple clicks to expand
ux_reasoning:
Code review has competing needs: see CHANGES (the +/- lines) and see CONTEXT (what's around the changes). The viewer must give both, in a layout that scales to 1000-line diffs.

Default display: 'collapsed context' — for each hunk (contiguous change region), show 3 lines of context above + below the change. Lines outside hunks are collapsed into expandable bands ('Show 24 lines' button). This is GitHub's default and it's right.

Split vs inline: a single toggle in the file header switches between modes. Split: two columns (old | new), changes highlighted in matching rows. Inline: one column with - lines red, + lines green, interleaved. Both modes have the same hunk-collapsing.

Comments: clicking the line-number gutter on any line adds a comment. Comments render INLINE below the commented line (collapses the surrounding lines if needed). Multiple comments thread via reply. Comments are anchored to LINE + SIDE (old vs new), so they stay correct across new commits.

Syntax highlighting: language detected from file extension; rendered via Prism or Shiki at build/render time. Diff colors (red/green) are LAYERED OVER syntax colors via low-alpha overlays so syntax is still visible. This is the GitHub move; most clones do diff colors that wash out the syntax.

Navigation: file tree on left (collapsible). Within a file, j/k moves to next/prev change hunk. n/p moves to next/prev file. ? shows shortcut list. All keyboard-first.

Review state: each comment can be 'pending' (only visible to you) or 'submitted'. A review batches all your pending comments into a single submission. The pending count shows in the review button: 'Submit review (4 comments)'.

Motion: zero on scroll. Comment composer slides in below the line over 150ms ease-out. Hunk expand reveals lines instantly.

Rejected: a 'rich diff' for prose-heavy files. Useful for non-code, but for code reviewers, the source is the truth. Also rejected: AI-suggested fixes inline. Useful in some workflows; here it'd clutter the comment thread.

design_tokens.colors: Diff add bg: oklch(0.95 0.08 145 / 0.3). Diff add line-number bg: oklch(0.7 0.14 145 / 0.5). Diff remove bg: oklch(0.95 0.08 25 / 0.3). Diff remove line-number bg: oklch(0.7 0.16 25 / 0.5). Syntax: standard Prism palette. Comment bg: oklch(0.97 0.005 250).
design_tokens.typography: Code: 'JetBrains Mono', 'Berkeley Mono' 12.5px / 18px. Line numbers: tabular-nums 11px oklch(0.55). Comment body: Inter 13px / 20px.
design_tokens.spacing: Diff row: 18px tall. Line-number gutter: 48px wide. Comment indent: 64px from diff edge.
design_tokens.motion: Comment composer slide-in: 150ms ease-out. Hunk expand: instant. Mode toggle (split/inline): 200ms crossfade. NO scroll animations.

component_architecture: <DiffView<TFile extends DiffFile> files={...}> generic. <DiffFile file mode={'split'|'inline'} comments={...} />. <DiffHunk lines={...} />. <DiffLine line side onAddComment />. <InlineComment thread={...} />. Comment state lives in a ReviewContext that batches submissions.
interaction_details: Click any line number in the gutter: comment composer slides in below that line (150ms ease-out). Type, submit, comment appears inline.
Comments are anchored to (file path, line number, old/new side) — they stay correct across new commits.
Mode toggle (split/inline) in file header: 200ms crossfade between modes. Same data, two layouts.
j/k navigates between hunks within a file; n/p navigates between files. Standard GitHub keyboard model.
Syntax highlighting layers under diff colors at low alpha — red/green bgs don't wash out syntax.
accessibility_considerations: Line-number buttons have aria-label='Add comment on line N'. Diff lines use role='row'; file is <article>. Comment composer is a real <textarea> with proper label. Keyboard nav: full j/k/n/p model. Reduced-motion: composer slide becomes instant. Color: diff +/- supplemented by '+' / '-' character in first column AND aria-label.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Comments are inline INSIDE the diff at the commented line — never disconnected sidebar. 2) Hunk collapsing with expand bands shows 3 lines context by default but full context one click away — scalable to 1000-line diffs. 3) Diff colors layer over syntax via low-alpha overlays — both signals visible simultaneously, not one washing the other out.

title: Tag autocomplete with quoted phrases, AND/OR/NOT operators, and saved searches
design_brief: You're building search-with-filters for a bug tracker (think Linear's filter bar, Jira's JQL but human-friendly, Github's issue search). Power users want to compose complex queries from keyboard with autocomplete. Pain point: most filter UIs are dropdown-heavy (slow) or require learning JQL (steep). The bar: Linear's filter syntax (the best balance), GitHub's issue search, Slack's search modifiers.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Filter UI requiring 4 clicks per filter (open dropdown → category → value → apply)
Free-text search that doesn't understand modifiers
Generic 'is:open' syntax with no autocomplete
Saved searches as a separate page
Apply button required to commit each filter
No way to remove an applied filter from keyboard
ux_reasoning:
Filter search is fundamentally textual but power tools should auto-suggest at every step. The right model: one search input that autocompletes both PHRASE and FILTER syntax.

Syntax: free text (any unquoted word) becomes a fuzzy content search. 'Quoted phrase' becomes an exact match. Key:value pairs (status:open, assignee:@alice, label:'p1') filter by structured field. Operators: AND (default between terms), OR (explicit), NOT (-prefix or NOT keyword). Parentheses for grouping.

Autocomplete: as the user types, suggestions appear based on the current token's context. After 'status:' → suggest status values (open, in-progress, done). After 'assignee:' → suggest team members. After typing nothing → suggest common filters + recent searches.

Suggestion structure: dropdown below input, each row shows: icon (for field type), key:value pair, count of matching items. Arrow keys navigate, Tab or Enter accepts (Tab keeps typing, Enter commits search).

Applied filters visualization: as filters are committed, they render as inline pills WITHIN the input itself (Linear's move), not below it. The user types a filter, it converts to a pill, cursor advances. Backspace deletes the pill.

Saved searches: clicking the search icon (or pressing Cmd+S while focused) opens a small popover 'Save this search' with a name input. Saved searches appear in the autocomplete dropdown (top section) for any matching query.

Keyboard model: focus the input, type or paste. Arrow keys navigate suggestions. Tab inserts suggestion (without committing search). Enter commits search. Esc clears the input. Backspace at start removes last filter pill. Cmd+/ shows the syntax help.

Motion: filter pill conversion (text → pill): 80ms ease-out width transition. Suggestion dropdown appears in 80ms. NO spring on commit.

Rejected: a separate 'Filters' panel with checkboxes. Modal-style filter UI is slow for power users. Single input with autocomplete is the right scope. Also rejected: natural-language search ('show me Alice's bugs from last week'). NLP is brittle and unpredictable; structured syntax with great autocomplete is more honest.

design_tokens.colors: Input bg: white. Filter pill: oklch(0.94 0.04 250) bg, oklch(0.3 0.04 250) text. Pill operator (AND/OR/NOT): oklch(0.7 0.005 250) bg, oklch(0.3 0.005 250) text. Suggestion focused: oklch(0.96 0.005 250). Saved-search badge: oklch(0.95 0.08 60).
design_tokens.typography: Input + pills: Inter 14px. Suggestion items: 13px. Field key in pill: 12px medium. Value in pill: 12px regular. Counts in suggestions: 11px tabular-nums.
design_tokens.spacing: Input: 40px tall, full-width. Pill: 24px tall, 2px gap between. Suggestion dropdown: 360px max-height with scroll.
design_tokens.motion: Text-to-pill conversion: 80ms ease-out. Dropdown appear: 80ms opacity. Pill remove: 80ms opacity fade-out.

component_architecture: <FilterSearch /> root with <SearchInput /> + <SuggestionDropdown />. Filter tokens stored as discriminated union: TextToken | FilterToken | OperatorToken | GroupToken. Parser converts text to tokens on commit. Renderer mixes tokens with editable text within a contenteditable. useSuggestions(currentToken, schema) returns context-aware suggestions.
interaction_details: Type 'sta' → autocomplete shows 'status:'. Tab inserts. Type 'op' → autocomplete shows 'open'. Tab inserts. Press Enter to commit search.
Filter pills appear inline in the input as you type. Backspace at start of input removes the last pill.
Operators (AND, OR, NOT) render as smaller gray pills between filter pills.
Cmd+S saves current search; saved searches appear in autocomplete (top section) for future use.
Esc clears the input. Cmd+/ shows syntax help popover.
accessibility_considerations: Input has aria-controls + aria-activedescendant pointing at suggestions. Suggestion list uses role='listbox' / role='option'. Filter pills are aria-labelled ('Filter: status open'). Backspace-to-remove-pill has keyboard discovery via Cmd+/ help. Reduced-motion: pill-in animation becomes instant. Color: filter pills supplemented by field:value text content.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Filters render as inline pills WITHIN the input itself — not below, not separate. The input IS the filter bar. 2) Tab inserts suggestion without committing (keeps typing); Enter commits — separates exploration from execution. 3) Backspace at start of empty input removes the last pill — fast keyboard removal without reaching for X buttons.

title: Live slide presentation with audience sync, presenter notes, and stage time
design_brief: You're building the presenter view for a slide deck tool (think Pitch, Tome, Apple Keynote's presenter display). Speakers want presenter notes + next slide preview + stage timer + audience sync indicators all visible at once. Pain point: most presenter views show too much OR too little. The bar: Keynote's classic presenter display, Pitch's modern take, Loom's presenter mode.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Presenter view that just mirrors the audience view
Tiny presenter notes text (8pt)
Stage timer that counts down (anxiety) instead of counting up (orientation)
Loud animation when audience focus changes slides
Generic 'Next slide' button — should be space/right-arrow
No connection indicator for online audiences
ux_reasoning:
Presenter view is a cockpit. Every element earns its place. Priorities:

1. CURRENT SLIDE LARGE (60% of width) — what audience sees, mirrored
2. NEXT SLIDE PREVIEW (top-right, 25% width) — what's coming, so speaker can transition smoothly
3. PRESENTER NOTES (right side below preview, 25% width) — speaker's script
4. STAGE TIMER (top-left, large) — elapsed time, NOT countdown (count-up reduces anxiety)
5. SLIDE COUNTER (bottom-center) — '7 / 24'
6. AUDIENCE INDICATORS (bottom-right) — connected viewer count + active reactions

Timer behavior: starts at 00:00 when presentation begins. Counts UP. If target time is set, the timer text shifts to orange at 90% target, red at 100% target. NEVER blocks or interrupts — just changes color.

Keyboard model: space / right-arrow = next slide. Left-arrow / Shift+space = previous. B = blackout (audience screen goes black, speaker keeps presenter view — Keynote feature). W = whiteout. Esc = end presentation. T = open speaker timer reset.

Presenter notes: speaker-authored in deck editor, rendered in presenter view at 17px / 26px (readable from a distance). Notes can be bullets, paragraphs, or even include hints like '(pause for laughter)'.

Audience sync: WebRTC or websocket pushes slide changes to remote viewers. Viewers see only the audience view. A small dot indicator on the presenter view shows N viewers connected. If a viewer lags >2s behind: their dot turns yellow (sync warning) — but never block presenter advance.

Reactions: audience members can tap a heart, clap, or thumbs-up reaction (small floating icons on audience view + ephemeral counts in presenter view at bottom-right). Reactions are batched and shown as 'heart × 5' to avoid noise.

Motion: slide change in audience view is instant. Slide change in presenter view: next-slide preview slides into current-slide position over 200ms (subtle, communicates the transition). Timer updates: digit-roll style. Reactions appear with 200ms ease-out scale-in + 1500ms fade.

Rejected: AI live captions in presenter view. Latency makes them lag the speaker; useful in audience view, distracting in presenter. Also rejected: 'previous slide' preview — too much info, hurts the focus on next slide.

design_tokens.colors: Presenter bg: oklch(0.10 0.005 250). Slide bg: white. Timer normal: oklch(0.85 0.005 250). Timer warning (>90% target): oklch(0.75 0.16 60). Timer over: oklch(0.6 0.18 25). Audience dot connected: oklch(0.55 0.14 145). Audience dot lagging: oklch(0.75 0.16 80).
design_tokens.typography: Timer: 'JetBrains Mono' 32px tabular-nums. Slide counter: 14px. Presenter notes: 'Inter' 17px / 26px. Next slide label: 11px uppercase tracking-wider.
design_tokens.spacing: Current slide: 60vw × 60vh in viewport center. Next preview: 320px × 180px top-right. Notes: 380px wide bottom-right.
design_tokens.motion: Slide change in presenter (preview→current): 200ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). Timer digit roll: 200ms per digit. Audience slide change: instant.

component_architecture: <PresenterView /> renders the cockpit layout. <Slide /> renders one slide (rich content). <Timer startedAt targetSec /> shows elapsed with color states. <AudienceIndicators viewers={...} /> shows dots + reactions. <AudienceView /> is a separate component for remote viewers (mirror only). useSlidesSync() broadcasts slide changes via websocket.
interaction_details: Space or right-arrow: advance slide. Shift+space or left-arrow: previous. B: audience blackout (presenter keeps view).
Timer counts UP (not down) — orientation, not anxiety. Color shifts to orange at 90% target, red over target.
Next slide preview top-right always shows the upcoming slide.
Audience viewer dots: green = synced, yellow = lagging >2s. Never blocks presenter advance.
Reactions from audience: appear briefly as small icons bottom-right, batched (5 hearts → ❤×5 if needed).
accessibility_considerations: Timer has aria-label with verbal time. Slide content has aria-label='Current slide N'. Keyboard shortcuts documented in a press-? help overlay. Reduced-motion: preview slide-in and reaction scale-in become instant. Color: timer state supplemented by font weight or icon at warning/critical levels. Audience dots have aria-label with viewer count.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Timer counts UP (not down) — reduces presenter anxiety, gives orientation. Color states warn at thresholds without blocking. 2) Next-slide preview always visible — speakers can plan transitions verbally. 3) Audience viewer dots track per-viewer lag with sync-warning color — but never block presenter (advance always works).

title: Hover-preview link with shimmer-to-content swap and ESC cancellation
design_brief: You're building rich link previews for a docs/notes app (think Notion's @-mention preview, Linear's issue references, GitHub's PR hover cards). When users hover a link, a preview card appears with the destination's content. Pain point: most previews are either heavy (loads everything on hover, slow) or empty (shows just the URL). The bar: GitHub's hover cards, Linear's reference previews, Notion's mention popovers.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Preview that appears on hover after 100ms — too aggressive
Preview that's a tooltip with just the title
Loading spinner inside preview while content fetches
Preview that follows cursor (jumpy)
No way to cancel preview mid-fetch
Cached preview shown stale forever
ux_reasoning:
Hover previews should respect user intent. Brief hovers (scanning) should NOT trigger previews; deliberate hovers (intending to look) SHOULD.

Intent detection: preview triggers after 350ms of sustained hover (the 'intention' threshold). Earlier hover-leave aborts; cursor remains in the document undisturbed. This is GitHub's exact timing.

Fetch + display: at 350ms, fetch the destination's preview metadata (title, snippet, thumbnail, status). Show a skeleton card immediately (anchored to link, not cursor) while fetching. Skeleton matches the layout of the real preview to avoid layout shift on hydration.

ESC cancellation: if the user starts to hover but realizes they don't want the preview, Esc cancels the fetch + dismisses any visible preview. Same applies if user moves mouse away from BOTH the link AND the preview card (with a 300ms grace period to allow mouse travel into the preview).

Cache: previews cached per URL for 5 minutes. Hovering a previously-previewed link shows the cached version instantly + revalidates in background.

Preview content: title (link text-style), 2-3 line snippet, optional thumbnail (right side), metadata (author, last updated). For interactive destinations (a doc, an issue): includes a 'recently changed' indicator if updated in last 24h.

Positioning: smart placement — try below the link first; if no room, above. Card is 320px wide, ~120px tall depending on content. Never overlaps the link itself.

Motion: skeleton fade-in at 350ms over 80ms opacity. Hydration: instant text/image swap. Card dismiss: 100ms opacity fade-out.

Rejected: showing the preview on cursor proximity (within 100px of link). Tested it — users hate the surprise. Hover-target-only is the right model. Also rejected: 'pinned' previews that stay until clicked. Adds floating-window management; previews are transient.

design_tokens.colors: Card bg: white with 1px oklch(0.92 0.005 250) border + shadow. Title: oklch(0.18 0.005 250). Snippet: oklch(0.45 0.005 250). Recently-updated dot: oklch(0.55 0.18 250) 6px.
design_tokens.typography: Card title: Inter 14px medium. Snippet: 13px regular. Metadata: 11px regular oklch(0.55).
design_tokens.spacing: Card: 320px wide, 12px padding. Thumbnail: 64px square right-aligned with 8px gap.
design_tokens.motion: Skeleton appear: 80ms opacity at 350ms after hover. Hydration: instant swap. Dismiss: 100ms opacity fade-out.

component_architecture: <HoverPreviewLink href hoverDelay={350}> wraps a link. Internally manages intent timer + fetch + cache. <PreviewCard data status={'loading' | 'ready' | 'error'} anchorRef />. usePreviewCache() singleton stores previews for 5 minutes. AbortController used for in-flight cancellation.
interaction_details: Hover a link for 350ms: skeleton card appears below link. Fetch fires in background. Hydrates to real content as data arrives.
Move cursor away within 350ms: no preview triggered.
Move cursor away after preview opens: 300ms grace period for travel into the card; otherwise card dismisses.
Esc at any time: cancels in-flight fetch and dismisses card.
Cached preview within 5 min: shows instantly, no skeleton.
accessibility_considerations: Preview card uses role='dialog' with aria-label='Link preview'. Recently-updated dot has aria-label. For keyboard users: focus + Enter on link triggers preview after 350ms hold (or instant if cached). Reduced-motion: preview fade-in becomes instant. The actual link remains a real <a href>, so screen readers and keyboard nav work without the preview chrome.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) 350ms intent threshold matches GitHub's exact timing — brief scans don't trigger; deliberate hovers do. 2) Skeleton matches the real preview layout to avoid layout shift on hydration. 3) Esc cancels in-flight fetches and dismisses the card — user control over an ambient UI.

title: Calendar heatmap with intensity-aware tooltips and click-to-day drilldown
design_brief: You're building the contribution graph for a developer portfolio (think GitHub's contribution graph, but extended for any time-series count data). Used to visualize habits, contributions, sales, etc. Pain point: GitHub's chart is iconic but tooltips are weak (just count and date), and the color scale doesn't scale well to non-coding data with wildly different value ranges. The bar: GitHub's contribution graph, Wakatime's coding heatmap, Apple Fitness ring overview.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Linear (non-log) color scale that washes out for skewed data
Single column of weekdays without grouping by month
Tooltip 'X contributions on Y' (redundant)
Color scale that's the same green even for non-coding data (out of context)
Hover triggering immediate tooltip without 200ms delay
No way to click a day for detail
ux_reasoning:
GitHub's contribution graph works for code because contribution distributions are bursty (many days with 0, occasional spikes). Same chart with a linear scale for, say, sales data (smooth distribution) ends up all the same shade. The chart must adapt.

Scale: quantile-based (not linear). We bucket all non-zero values into 4 quantile buckets (25/50/75/100 percentile). Each bucket gets one of 4 color intensities. This means a heavily skewed distribution AND a smooth distribution both get usable visualization. Empty days are a 5th color (neutral gray).

Color theming: each instance can pick a hue family — green for GitHub-style, blue for water consumption, red for warnings/incidents. The 4 intensities are computed in OKLCH for perceptual uniformity (L: 0.92, 0.78, 0.6, 0.42; C: 0.04, 0.08, 0.14, 0.18; constant H).

Layout: 7 rows × N week columns. Month labels above the columns where months change. Day-of-week labels on left (M, W, F shown to save space). This is GitHub's exact layout.

Tooltip: appears on hover after 200ms (intent threshold like the hover preview). Shows: full date, count, AND comparative context ('3 contributions — 2nd most active Tuesday this year'). The comparative info is computed at render time from the full dataset. This is the killer move: tooltip is analysis, not data dump.

Click a day: opens a side panel with that day's detail (a list of contributions, sales, whatever the data represents). Side panel slides in from right, doesn't dim the chart. Esc closes.

Month-level summary: hovering a month label shows that month's total + comparison ('342 contributions in March — up 12% from February').

Motion: cell hover bg shift is instant. Tooltip fade-in is 80ms. Side panel slide is 250ms ease-out.

Rejected: animations of cells filling in on initial load. Adds 2 seconds of choreography for a chart users want immediately. Also rejected: pan/zoom on the heatmap. Year view is the entire data; smaller windows are a tabular detail panel, not the heatmap.

design_tokens.colors: 4-intensity scale (green family example): bucket 1 oklch(0.92 0.04 145), 2 oklch(0.78 0.08 145), 3 oklch(0.6 0.14 145), 4 oklch(0.42 0.18 145). Empty days: oklch(0.95 0.005 250). Cell hover outline: 1px oklch(0.2 0.01 250).
design_tokens.typography: Day-of-week labels: 10px regular oklch(0.55). Month labels: 11px medium. Tooltip body: 13px regular.
design_tokens.spacing: Cell: 11px square with 2px gap (GitHub's exact size). Total chart: ~720px wide × ~110px tall.
design_tokens.motion: Cell hover outline: instant. Tooltip fade-in (200ms delay then 80ms fade): minimal. Side panel slide: 250ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1).

component_architecture: <CalendarHeatmap<TDayData> data hue colorBuckets onDayClick> generic. <Cell day intensity onHover />. <Tooltip day stats /> with intent-aware show. computeQuantiles() runs at render to bucket values.
interaction_details: Hover a cell for 200ms: tooltip appears with date, count, and comparative analysis ('#2 most active Tuesday this year').
Quantile-based color buckets (25/50/75/100) — handles both bursty and smooth distributions usable.
Hue is themable (default green for code, but blue/red/purple for other domains).
Click a day: opens side panel with that day's detail (list of events/contributions/whatever).
Cell focus: 1px outline. Tab cycles through days; Enter opens detail.
accessibility_considerations: role='figure' on chart with aria-label. Each cell is a real <button> with aria-label='date: count'. Tooltip uses role='status' aria-live='polite' but only on intentional hover (200ms delay). Keyboard: Tab cycles cells; Enter opens day detail; arrow keys navigate by day or week. Reduced-motion: tooltip fade-in becomes instant. Color: intensity is supplemented by aria-label exact count.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Quantile-based color buckets (not linear) — handles both bursty distributions (code commits) and smooth ones (sales) with the same usability. 2) Tooltip includes comparative analysis ('#2 most active Tuesday this year'), not just the cell value — adds insight on hover. 3) Hue is themable so the same component visualizes coding, water consumption, incidents, etc. — without losing its identity.

title: Per-feature 'try it' inline simulator that lets users practice without consequences
design_brief: You're building feature discovery for a powerful but intimidating tool (think Adobe Illustrator, Excel, Figma's pen tool). Pain point: tooltips explain features but users don't learn until they DO. Real practice is scary because they might break something. The bar: Photoshop's Learn section, Figma's interactive tutorials, Khan Academy's practice-mode lessons.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Tutorial video that takes 4 minutes to explain a 5-second action
Modal practice mode that's separate from the real UI
Disabled real features during tutorial
Generic 'good job!' affirmations
Forced ordering of lessons
No way to skip the practice
ux_reasoning:
The hardest features to onboard are 'powerful primitives' (pen tool, regex, formulas) that require experimentation. The ideal onboarding: a SANDBOX version of the feature that's safe to break, available inline where the feature lives.

Mechanics: when a user opens a complex tool panel (e.g., the formula editor in a spreadsheet, or the pen tool in a vector editor), a small 'Try it' badge appears in the panel's corner. Clicking opens a sandbox: a mini-canvas/mini-spreadsheet with sample data, alongside the real tool. The user can experiment freely; nothing they do affects their real work.

The sandbox is the WHOLE tutorial: no text, no narration, no 'do step 1 now'. The user just plays. Each sandbox includes 2-3 example problems ('Try drawing a curve', 'Try summing this column') as ghost text in the sandbox; clicking an example loads it as a starting point.

'Done' detection: when the user has performed the feature's core action successfully (e.g., drawn a closed path, entered a valid formula), a small inline checkmark + 'Got it' button appears. Clicking 'Got it' closes the sandbox AND marks the feature as understood. The Try-it badge disappears for that feature permanently.

Return anytime: even after dismissal, a 'Practice' link lives in the panel header. Users can re-open the sandbox whenever.

Progressive complexity: each feature's sandbox has 3 example problems sorted by complexity. Most users complete the first; some explore further. The sandbox doesn't force progression.

Motion: sandbox open/close: 200ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1) height transition. Success checkmark draws in over 300ms.

Rejected: a top-level 'Learn' menu with all tutorials. Disconnected from where features live. Inline sandboxes meet users at the point of need. Also rejected: gamification with progress bars and badges. Hostile to professional users; treats them like Duolingo learners.

design_tokens.colors: Try-it badge: oklch(0.55 0.18 250) bg, white text. Sandbox bg: oklch(0.97 0.04 250) at 30% (subtle blue tint to distinguish from real workspace). Example ghost text: oklch(0.6 0.005 250) at 50%. Success checkmark: oklch(0.55 0.14 145).
design_tokens.typography: Try-it badge: 11px uppercase tracking-wider. Sandbox label: 11px uppercase tracking-wider in oklch(0.55). Examples: italic 12px.
design_tokens.spacing: Sandbox: 240px height inline below the tool panel. Padding: 12px. Examples list: 4px gap.
design_tokens.motion: Sandbox open: 200ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1) height. Success checkmark draw: 300ms ease-out stroke-dasharray. NO confetti.

component_architecture: <TryItSandbox featureId children examples /> wraps the real tool panel. Internally tracks completion state in localStorage. Sandbox content is fully isolated (separate React state, separate data — never the user's real data). useSandboxCompletion(featureId) hook manages persistence.
interaction_details: Open the formula editor: a 'TRY IT' badge appears in its corner. Click → sandbox opens below with a mini-spreadsheet.
Sandbox is fully isolated — nothing the user does affects their real spreadsheet.
Example problems shown as italic links. Click any to load that example as starting state.
Successful action (e.g., valid formula entered): inline checkmark + 'Got it' button appears. Click Got it: dismisses badge permanently.
Even after dismissal: a small 'Practice' link in the panel header re-opens the sandbox.
accessibility_considerations: Sandbox is role='region' with descriptive aria-label. Success message uses role='status' aria-live='polite'. Examples are real <button>s with clear labels. Reduced-motion: sandbox open and checkmark animations become instant. Keyboard: Tab into sandbox; sandbox itself has its own keyboard model matching the real tool.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Sandbox is INLINE with the real tool, not a separate page — practice and use share the same surface. 2) No narration, no 'step 1 → step 2' — the user just plays. 3) Completion is signaled by ACTION (valid formula entered, closed path drawn), not by clicking 'Next' — the user proves they got it by doing it.
